title: Notice of Privacy Practices (NPP)
description: Required HIPAA notice describing how [PRACTICE NAME] uses and discloses PHI and patient rights.
bodyMarkdown:
## Notice of Privacy Practices

**[PRACTICE NAME]** is committed to protecting the privacy of your health information. This notice describes how we may use and disclose your protected health information (PHI) and your rights regarding that information.

### How We Use and Disclose Your Health Information

- Treatment: We may use your PHI to provide, coordinate, or manage your healthcare.

- Payment: We may use your PHI to bill and collect payment for services.

- Healthcare Operations: We may use your PHI for quality improvement, training, and administrative functions.

### Your Rights

- Right to inspect and copy your health records

- Right to request an amendment to your health records

- Right to request restrictions on uses and disclosures

- Right to receive confidential communications

- Right to an accounting of disclosures

- Right to receive a paper copy of this notice

### Effective Date

This notice is effective as of [DATE]. [PRACTICE NAME] reserves the right to change this notice and make the revised notice effective for health information we already have as well as any information we receive in the future.

Complaints may be filed with the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Office for Civil Rights.

title: Minimum Necessary Standard Policy
description: Governs how [PRACTICE NAME] limits PHI use and disclosure to the minimum necessary.
bodyMarkdown:
## Minimum Necessary Standard Policy

**Policy:** [PRACTICE NAME] shall make reasonable efforts to limit the use, disclosure, and requests for PHI to the minimum amount necessary to accomplish the intended purpose.

### Scope

This policy applies to all workforce members who access, use, or disclose PHI.

### Standard Uses and Disclosures

The Privacy Officer shall identify classes of workforce members who need access to PHI to carry out their job duties and define the category of PHI each class needs. Access shall be role-based and limited to what is necessary.

### Non-Routine Disclosures

For non-routine disclosures, the workforce member shall make an individual determination that the amount of information disclosed is limited to the minimum necessary.

### Exceptions

The minimum necessary standard does not apply to: disclosures to treating providers, disclosures to patients themselves, uses or disclosures made pursuant to an authorization, disclosures required by law, or disclosures to HHS.

title: Patient Rights Policy
description: Defines the rights of patients regarding their PHI at [PRACTICE NAME].
bodyMarkdown:
## Patient Rights Policy

[PRACTICE NAME] recognizes and protects the following patient rights under HIPAA (45 CFR §164.520–164.528):

### Right of Access (45 CFR §164.524)

Patients have the right to inspect and obtain a copy of their PHI in a designated record set. Requests must be fulfilled within 30 days. Fees may be charged for copies as permitted by applicable law.

### Right to Amend (45 CFR §164.526)

Patients may request amendments to their PHI. [PRACTICE NAME] shall act on such requests within 60 days.

### Right to Accounting of Disclosures (45 CFR §164.528)

Patients may request an accounting of disclosures made in the 6 years prior to the request date, excluding disclosures for treatment, payment, and operations.

### Right to Restrict (45 CFR §164.522)

Patients may request restrictions on PHI uses and disclosures. [PRACTICE NAME] must agree to requests to restrict disclosures to health plans when the patient pays out-of-pocket in full.

### Right to Confidential Communications

Patients may request to receive communications by alternative means or at alternative locations.

title: Access to Records and Record Release Policy
description: Procedures for providing patients timely access to their health records.
bodyMarkdown:
## Access to Records and Record Release Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for responding to patient requests for access to their PHI in compliance with 45 CFR §164.524.

### Receiving Requests

All requests for records access must be submitted in writing using [PRACTICE NAME]'s standard Authorization for Release of Records form. Verbal requests are acceptable for inspection only.

### Response Timeline

- Standard: Within 30 days of receiving the request

- Extension: One 30-day extension permitted with written notice

- Electronic PHI: Must be provided in the format requested by the individual if readily producible

### Fees

Fees shall be limited to the cost of labor for copying, supplies, and postage as permitted under [STATE] law. No retrieval fees may be charged for electronic records.

### Denial of Access

Access may be denied only in limited circumstances defined under 45 CFR §164.524(a)(2). Denials must be documented and provided in writing with instructions for review.

title: Amendment of PHI Policy
description: Procedures for patients to request amendments to their health information.
bodyMarkdown:
## Amendment of PHI Policy

**Purpose:** To establish a process for patients to request amendments to their PHI per 45 CFR §164.526.

### Submitting an Amendment Request

Patients must submit amendment requests in writing, identifying the specific PHI to be amended and the reason for the amendment. Requests are submitted to the Privacy Officer at [PRACTICE NAME].

### Review and Decision

[PRACTICE NAME] shall act on an amendment request within 60 days of receipt. A 30-day extension is permitted with written notice.

### Grounds for Denial

Amendments may be denied if the PHI was not created by [PRACTICE NAME], is not part of the designated record set, is not available for inspection, or is already accurate and complete.

### Acceptance

When an amendment is accepted, [PRACTICE NAME] shall: append or link the amendment, inform the patient, and notify relevant persons who received the original PHI.

title: Accounting of Disclosures Policy
description: Procedures for tracking and reporting disclosures of PHI outside routine operations.
bodyMarkdown:
## Accounting of Disclosures Policy

**Purpose:** To track disclosures of PHI made without authorization for purposes other than treatment, payment, and healthcare operations, and to provide patients with an accounting upon request per 45 CFR §164.528.

### Disclosures That Must Be Tracked

- Disclosures required by law

- Disclosures for public health activities

- Disclosures about victims of abuse, neglect, or domestic violence

- Disclosures for health oversight activities

- Disclosures for judicial and administrative proceedings

- Disclosures for law enforcement purposes

- Disclosures for research (without authorization)

### Information to Record

For each disclosure: date, name and address of the recipient, description of PHI disclosed, and purpose of disclosure.

### Retention

Disclosure logs shall be retained for 6 years.

title: Restriction Requests Policy
description: Policy for handling patient requests to restrict uses or disclosures of PHI.
bodyMarkdown:
## Restriction Requests Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for receiving and responding to patient requests for restrictions on PHI uses and disclosures per 45 CFR §164.522.

### Required Restrictions

[PRACTICE NAME] must agree to a patient's request to restrict disclosure to a health plan when the patient or their representative pays out-of-pocket in full for the services and the disclosure would be made solely for payment or operations.

### Voluntary Restrictions

[PRACTICE NAME] may, but is not required to, agree to other restriction requests. If agreed to, the restriction must be honored except for emergency treatment.

### Terminating a Restriction

Restrictions may be terminated by written agreement of both parties or by [PRACTICE NAME] with written notice to the patient (effective only for future uses and disclosures).

title: Confidential Communications Policy
description: Policy for accommodating patient requests for confidential communications.
bodyMarkdown:
## Confidential Communications Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for accommodating reasonable requests by patients to receive communications of PHI by alternative means or at alternative locations per 45 CFR §164.522(b).

### Requests

Patients may request that [PRACTICE NAME] communicate with them using a specific phone number, address, or other method. Patients are not required to provide a reason for their request.

### Reasonable Accommodation

[PRACTICE NAME] must accommodate reasonable requests. Requests may be denied only if they would not be possible or would significantly impede operations.

### Implementation

Staff shall document all approved confidential communication requests in the patient record and update scheduling and billing systems accordingly.

title: Marketing and Fundraising Policy
description: Governs use of PHI for marketing and fundraising communications.
bodyMarkdown:
## Marketing and Fundraising Policy

**Purpose:** To ensure [PRACTICE NAME] obtains appropriate authorization before using PHI for marketing and follows HIPAA rules for fundraising communications.

### Marketing Definition

Marketing is any communication about a product or service that encourages recipients to purchase or use the product or service and where [PRACTICE NAME] receives financial remuneration from a third party.

### Authorization Required

PHI may not be used for marketing without a valid HIPAA-compliant written authorization, except for communications describing health-related products or services provided by [PRACTICE NAME] and face-to-face communications.

### Fundraising

If [PRACTICE NAME] conducts fundraising, patients must be given a clear opportunity to opt out of receiving fundraising communications. Opt-out requests must be honored immediately.

title: Research Authorization Policy
description: Policy governing PHI use in research activities.
bodyMarkdown:
## Research Authorization Policy

**Purpose:** To ensure all research activities using PHI comply with HIPAA Privacy Rule requirements under 45 CFR §164.512(i).

### Authorization

PHI may be used or disclosed for research with a valid patient authorization. The authorization must describe the research purpose, the PHI to be used, who will receive the PHI, and expiration date or event.

### Waiver of Authorization

A waiver of authorization may be granted by an IRB or Privacy Board if: the research could not practicably be conducted without the waiver; the research could not practicably be conducted without access to the PHI; and the privacy risks are minimal.

### Preparatory Research

PHI may be reviewed for research preparatory purposes without authorization if the researcher represents that the PHI will not leave [PRACTICE NAME] and is necessary for research preparation.

title: Decedent PHI Policy
description: Policy for handling PHI of deceased patients.
bodyMarkdown:
## Decedent PHI Policy

**Purpose:** To establish guidelines for the use and disclosure of PHI of deceased individuals in accordance with 45 CFR §164.502(f).

### General Rule

[PRACTICE NAME] shall protect the PHI of deceased individuals for 50 years following the date of death.

### Authorized Disclosures

PHI of a deceased individual may be disclosed to a personal representative (e.g., executor, administrator) or to family members and others involved in the care of the deceased, when consistent with any prior expressed preference and if not contrary to applicable law.

### Law Enforcement and Public Health

PHI may be disclosed to law enforcement officials or public health authorities as permitted under applicable law and the HIPAA Privacy Rule.

title: Disaster Relief and Emergency Disclosure Policy
description: Governs PHI disclosures during disasters and public health emergencies.
bodyMarkdown:
## Disaster Relief and Emergency Disclosure Policy

**Purpose:** To establish protocols for PHI disclosure during disasters and public health emergencies under 45 CFR §164.510(b) and §164.512.

### Permitted Disclosures

During a disaster or declared public health emergency, [PRACTICE NAME] may disclose PHI to: disaster relief organizations (e.g., Red Cross) for notification of family members; public health authorities; and government agencies as required by law.

### Minimum Necessary

Even during emergencies, the minimum necessary standard applies unless the disclosure is for treatment purposes.

### Documentation

All emergency disclosures must be documented in the patient record including the name of the recipient, date, and nature of information disclosed.

title: Undercover Investigations and Law Enforcement Policy
description: Policy for handling law enforcement requests for PHI.
bodyMarkdown:
## Law Enforcement and Undercover Investigations Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for responding to law enforcement requests for PHI under 45 CFR §164.512(f).

### Required Disclosures

[PRACTICE NAME] shall disclose PHI to law enforcement when required by law (e.g., mandatory reporting of gunshot wounds, abuse) or pursuant to a court order, warrant, subpoena, or administrative request.

### Permitted Disclosures

PHI may be disclosed to law enforcement to: identify or locate a suspect, material witness, or missing person; respond to a request related to a victim of a crime; or alert law enforcement to a crime on premises.

### Verification

Before disclosing PHI, staff shall verify the identity of the law enforcement official and the legal basis for the request. All requests and disclosures shall be documented.

title: Patient Privacy Complaint Policy
description: Procedures for receiving and resolving patient privacy complaints.
bodyMarkdown:
## Patient Privacy Complaint Policy

**Purpose:** To establish a process for patients to file complaints about [PRACTICE NAME]'s privacy practices per 45 CFR §164.530(d).

### Filing a Complaint

Patients may file a written or verbal complaint with the Privacy Officer at [PRACTICE NAME]. Complaints may also be filed with HHS Office for Civil Rights at www.hhs.gov/ocr/privacy.

### Non-Retaliation

[PRACTICE NAME] shall not intimidate, threaten, coerce, discriminate against, or take retaliatory action against any individual for exercising their privacy rights or filing a complaint.

### Investigation

The Privacy Officer shall investigate all complaints and document findings. Corrective action shall be taken as warranted. Patients shall receive a written response within 30 days.

title: Risk Analysis Policy
description: Policy for conducting annual HIPAA Security Rule risk analyses.
bodyMarkdown:
## Risk Analysis Policy

**Purpose:** To ensure [PRACTICE NAME] conducts accurate and thorough risk analyses of PHI confidentiality, integrity, and availability per 45 CFR §164.308(a)(1)(ii)(A).

### Frequency

A comprehensive risk analysis shall be conducted: (1) at least annually, (2) when new technology is implemented, (3) when operational changes occur, and (4) following a security incident.

### Scope

The risk analysis shall identify: all ePHI created, received, maintained, or transmitted; potential threats and vulnerabilities; current security controls; likelihood and impact of each threat; and level of risk.

### Documentation

Risk analysis results shall be documented, reviewed by leadership, and retained for 6 years. Identified risks shall be addressed through the Risk Management Policy.

title: Risk Management Policy
description: Policy for implementing security measures to reduce PHI risks to a reasonable level.
bodyMarkdown:
## Risk Management Policy

**Purpose:** To implement security measures sufficient to reduce risks and vulnerabilities to ePHI to a reasonable and appropriate level per 45 CFR §164.308(a)(1)(ii)(B).

### Risk Response

Following each risk analysis, [PRACTICE NAME] shall develop and implement a risk management plan that addresses identified risks through: risk avoidance, risk mitigation, risk transference, or risk acceptance with documented justification.

### Corrective Action Plans

For each identified risk rated MEDIUM or higher, a corrective action plan (CAP) shall be created with assigned owner, remediation steps, and target completion date.

### Monitoring

The Security Officer shall review CAP status quarterly and report to leadership. All risk management activities shall be documented and retained for 6 years.

title: Sanction Policy for Security Violations
description: Defines sanctions for workforce members who violate HIPAA security policies.
bodyMarkdown:
## Sanction Policy for Security Violations

**Purpose:** To establish sanctions for workforce members who violate [PRACTICE NAME]'s HIPAA Security Rule policies per 45 CFR §164.308(a)(1)(ii)(C).

### Sanction Levels

- Level 1 — Minor Violations: Verbal warning, additional training required

- Level 2 — Moderate Violations: Written warning, retraining, increased supervision

- Level 3 — Serious Violations: Suspension, loss of system access, termination

- Level 4 — Criminal Activity: Immediate termination, referral to law enforcement

### Factors Considered

Sanctions consider: severity and nature of the violation, whether the violation was intentional, harm caused, prior violations, and cooperation in investigation.

### Documentation

All sanction decisions shall be documented in the workforce member's personnel file.

title: Information System Activity Review Policy
description: Policy for regularly reviewing audit logs and system activity for security.
bodyMarkdown:
## Information System Activity Review Policy

**Purpose:** To implement procedures to regularly review records of ePHI system activity per 45 CFR §164.308(a)(1)(ii)(D).

### Review Frequency

System activity logs shall be reviewed: weekly for high-risk systems, monthly for standard systems, and immediately upon detection of a potential security incident.

### Items to Review

- Login and logout attempts (including failures)

- Access to patient records

- Changes to user privileges

- System alerts and error logs

- Remote access sessions

### Anomaly Response

Any anomalous activity shall be investigated by the Security Officer within 24 hours. Potential security incidents shall trigger the Incident Response Policy.

title: Workforce Clearance and Authorization Policy
description: Procedures to ensure only authorized workforce members access ePHI.
bodyMarkdown:
## Workforce Clearance and Authorization Policy

**Purpose:** To implement procedures to determine appropriate access to ePHI for each workforce member per 45 CFR §164.308(a)(3).

### Access Determination

Access to ePHI shall be determined based on: the workforce member's job function, minimum necessary requirements, and successful completion of HIPAA training.

### Clearance Process

Prior to granting ePHI access: (1) conduct background check, (2) verify credentials, (3) complete HIPAA Security awareness training, (4) sign confidentiality agreement.

### Role-Based Access

Access privileges shall be role-based. Access levels shall be reviewed annually and upon any change in job duties.

title: Workforce Termination Procedures
description: Security procedures for terminating workforce member access to ePHI systems.
bodyMarkdown:
## Workforce Termination Procedures

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for terminating access to ePHI when a workforce member's employment ends per 45 CFR §164.308(a)(3)(ii)(C).

### Immediate Actions (Upon Notice of Termination)

- Disable all system accounts and access credentials

- Revoke VPN and remote access

- Collect all access cards, keys, and devices

- Change shared passwords known to the terminated employee

- Disable email account or forward to supervisor

### For Involuntary Termination

System access shall be revoked simultaneously with or immediately before notifying the workforce member of termination.

### Documentation

All termination access revocations shall be documented and retained for 6 years.

title: Workstation Use and Security Policy
description: Policy governing acceptable use and physical security of workstations accessing ePHI.
bodyMarkdown:
## Workstation Use and Security Policy

**Purpose:** To specify proper functions and physical attributes for workstations accessing ePHI per 45 CFR §164.310(b) and (c).

### Acceptable Use

Workstations accessing ePHI shall be used only for work-related purposes. Personal use is prohibited on clinical workstations.

### Physical Security

- Position screens to prevent unauthorized viewing

- Use screen locks activating after 15 minutes of inactivity

- Log off or lock workstation when leaving for any period

- Never leave workstations unattended while logged in

### Home and Remote Workstations

Remote workstations accessing ePHI must be approved, encrypted, use VPN, have up-to-date antivirus software, and be located in a private, secure area.

### Third-Party IT Management

If workstation setup, security, or maintenance is managed by an external IT vendor or managed service provider (MSP), a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) must be in place with that vendor. The vendor must be added to the practice's **Vendor Management** list and assessed for risk. Verify that the vendor's remote access methods comply with this policy's encryption and authentication requirements.

title: Device and Media Controls Policy
description: Policy for secure handling of devices and media containing ePHI.
bodyMarkdown:
## Device and Media Controls Policy

**Purpose:** To govern the receipt and removal of hardware and electronic media containing ePHI per 45 CFR §164.310(d).

### Inventory

[PRACTICE NAME] shall maintain an inventory of all hardware and electronic media that stores ePHI. The inventory shall be reviewed quarterly.

### Media Reuse

Before electronic media is reused, all ePHI must be securely removed using NIST SP 800-88 compliant methods (overwriting, degaussing, or encryption).

### Disposal

Electronic media must be destroyed or rendered unreadable before disposal. Paper records containing PHI must be shredded using a cross-cut shredder.

### Movement

Movement of hardware or electronic media containing ePHI must be logged. Portable media (USB drives, laptops) must be encrypted.

### Third-Party IT Management

If device provisioning, decommissioning, or media destruction is handled by an external IT vendor, a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) must be in place. The vendor must be listed in the practice's **Vendor Management** module with documented risk assessment. Ensure the vendor follows NIST SP 800-88 compliant destruction methods and provides certificates of destruction.

title: Access Control Policy
description: Policy for granting unique user-based access to ePHI systems.
bodyMarkdown:
## Access Control Policy

**Purpose:** To implement technical policies ensuring only authorized persons access ePHI per 45 CFR §164.312(a).

### Unique User Identification

Each workforce member shall have a unique username. Shared accounts are prohibited on systems containing ePHI.

### Automatic Logoff

All systems containing ePHI shall be configured to automatically log off after 15 minutes of inactivity.

### Emergency Access

An emergency access procedure shall be established and documented to ensure access to ePHI during system emergencies. Emergency access events shall be logged.

### Encryption

ePHI transmitted over open networks must be encrypted using industry-standard encryption (AES-256 or equivalent). ePHI at rest on portable devices must be encrypted.

### Third-Party IT Management

If access control systems (Active Directory, identity management, EHR user provisioning) are managed by a third-party IT vendor or MSP, a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) must be in place. The vendor must be added to the practice's **Vendor Management** module. Review vendor access logs periodically to ensure they follow the principle of least privilege when accessing systems containing ePHI.

title: Audit Controls Policy
description: Policy for recording and examining system activity in systems containing ePHI.
bodyMarkdown:
## Audit Controls Policy

**Purpose:** To implement hardware, software, and procedural mechanisms that record and examine activity in systems containing ePHI per 45 CFR §164.312(b).

### Audit Log Requirements

All systems containing ePHI shall capture audit logs recording: user identity, date/time of access, actions performed (view, create, modify, delete), and whether the action succeeded or failed.

### Log Retention

Audit logs shall be retained for a minimum of 6 years.

### Log Protection

Audit logs shall be protected from unauthorized access or modification. Workforce members shall not have the ability to alter logs of their own activity.

### Review

Audit logs shall be reviewed per the Information System Activity Review Policy.

title: Integrity Controls Policy
description: Policy for protecting ePHI from improper alteration or destruction.
bodyMarkdown:
## Integrity Controls Policy

**Purpose:** To implement policies and procedures to protect ePHI from improper alteration or destruction per 45 CFR §164.312(c).

### Technical Controls

[PRACTICE NAME] shall implement technical security measures such as checksums, hash functions, or digital signatures to confirm ePHI has not been improperly altered or destroyed.

### Backup and Recovery

ePHI shall be backed up daily. Backups shall be tested monthly to verify integrity and restorability. Backup media shall be stored offsite or in a secure cloud environment.

### Authentication

Electronic mechanisms shall be implemented to corroborate that ePHI has not been altered or destroyed in an unauthorized manner.

title: Authentication and Transmission Security Policy
description: Policy for user authentication and secure transmission of ePHI.
bodyMarkdown:
## Authentication and Transmission Security Policy

**Purpose:** To implement technical security measures to guard against unauthorized access to ePHI transmitted over electronic communications networks per 45 CFR §164.312(d) and (e).

### Password Requirements

- Minimum 12 characters including uppercase, lowercase, number, and symbol

- Passwords must be changed every 90 days

- Previous 10 passwords may not be reused

- Multi-factor authentication (MFA) required for all remote access

### Transmission Encryption

All ePHI transmitted over public networks must use TLS 1.2 or higher. Email containing PHI must be encrypted. Unencrypted email is not an acceptable transmission method for PHI.

title: Business Associate Security Management Policy
description: Policy for managing security requirements of business associates.
bodyMarkdown:
## Business Associate Security Management Policy

**Purpose:** To ensure business associates of [PRACTICE NAME] provide adequate safeguards for PHI per 45 CFR §164.308(b).

### BAA Requirement

A Business Associate Agreement (BAA) must be executed before any business associate may access, receive, create, or maintain PHI on behalf of [PRACTICE NAME].

### BAA Contents

Each BAA must specify permitted uses and disclosures of PHI, require appropriate safeguards, require reporting of security incidents and breaches, require compliance by subcontractors, and provide for termination upon breach of the agreement.

### Monitoring

The Privacy/Security Officer shall maintain a current list of all business associates and review BAAs annually. BAAs shall be updated when there are material changes to the relationship.

title: Contingency Planning and Disaster Recovery Policy
description: Policy for establishing and testing a contingency plan for ePHI availability.
bodyMarkdown:
## Contingency Planning and Disaster Recovery Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for responding to an emergency or other occurrence that damages systems containing ePHI per 45 CFR §164.308(a)(7).

### Data Backup Plan

ePHI shall be backed up daily to an offsite or cloud location. Backups shall be encrypted. The backup process shall be automated and verified weekly.

### Disaster Recovery Plan

The disaster recovery plan shall document: recovery time objective (RTO), recovery point objective (RPO), system restoration procedures, and roles and responsibilities.

### Testing

The disaster recovery plan shall be tested at least annually. Test results and any corrective actions shall be documented.

title: Emergency Access Procedure
description: Procedures for obtaining access to ePHI during system outages or emergencies.
bodyMarkdown:
## Emergency Access Procedure

**Purpose:** To establish procedures to obtain necessary ePHI during an emergency per 45 CFR §164.312(a)(2)(ii).

### Emergency Access Methods

- Break-glass accounts: Pre-configured emergency accounts stored securely with the Security Officer

- Paper-based backup: Critical patient data printed and stored in locked emergency binder

- Vendor emergency access: Coordinated with EHR vendor for system-level emergency access

### Authorization

Emergency access must be authorized by the Security Officer or designee. All emergency access events shall be logged and reviewed within 24 hours of the emergency end.

title: Security Testing and Revision Policy
description: Policy for periodically testing and revising security policies and procedures.
bodyMarkdown:
## Security Testing and Revision Policy

**Purpose:** To implement procedures for periodic technical and non-technical testing of security measures per 45 CFR §164.306(e).

### Annual Review

All HIPAA Security policies shall be reviewed annually by the Security Officer and updated as needed based on: risk analysis results, security incidents, regulatory changes, and technology changes.

### Technical Testing

[PRACTICE NAME] shall conduct periodic vulnerability assessments and, for larger practices, penetration testing to evaluate the effectiveness of technical security controls.

### Policy Updates

Updated policies shall be distributed to all affected workforce members. Training shall be provided when significant changes are made.

title: Breach Assessment Policy
description: Policy for assessing whether an impermissible PHI disclosure constitutes a reportable breach.
bodyMarkdown:
## Breach Assessment Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for assessing whether an impermissible use or disclosure of PHI constitutes a breach requiring notification under 45 CFR §164.402.

### Presumption of Breach

An impermissible use or disclosure of PHI is presumed to be a breach unless [PRACTICE NAME] demonstrates a low probability that PHI has been compromised based on a four-factor risk assessment.

### Four-Factor Risk Assessment

1. The nature and extent of the PHI involved (type, amount, sensitivity)

2. The person who used or received the PHI and their obligations

3. Whether the PHI was actually acquired or viewed

4. The extent to which the risk has been mitigated

### Documentation

All breach risk assessments must be documented regardless of outcome. Documentation shall be retained for 6 years.

title: Individual Breach Notification Policy
description: Procedures for notifying affected individuals of a reportable PHI breach.
bodyMarkdown:
## Individual Breach Notification Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for notifying individuals when their PHI is the subject of a breach per 45 CFR §164.404.

### Timing

Individual notification must be provided without unreasonable delay and in no case later than 60 days following discovery of the breach.

### Method of Notification

Notification shall be provided by first-class mail to the individual's last known address. Electronic notification may be used if the individual has agreed to receive notices electronically.

### Content of Notification

The notification must include: description of the breach, types of PHI involved, steps individuals should take to protect themselves, steps [PRACTICE NAME] is taking, and contact information.

title: Media Notification Policy
description: Policy for notifying media outlets of breaches affecting 500+ residents in a state.
bodyMarkdown:
## Media Notification Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for notifying prominent media outlets when a breach affects 500 or more residents of a state or jurisdiction per 45 CFR §164.406.

### Threshold

If a breach affects 500 or more residents of a state or jurisdiction, [PRACTICE NAME] shall notify prominent media outlets serving that area.

### Timing

Media notification must be provided without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 days following discovery.

### Content

Media notification shall include the same information as required in individual notices (description, PHI types, protective steps, contact information).

title: HHS Breach Notification Policy
description: Policy for notifying HHS of reportable PHI breaches.
bodyMarkdown:
## HHS Breach Notification Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for notifying HHS of reportable breaches per 45 CFR §164.408.

### Breaches Affecting 500+ Individuals

For breaches affecting 500 or more individuals, [PRACTICE NAME] shall notify HHS contemporaneously with individual notification (within 60 days of discovery) through the HHS web portal at www.hhs.gov/hipaa.

### Breaches Affecting Fewer Than 500 Individuals

For breaches affecting fewer than 500 individuals, [PRACTICE NAME] shall maintain a log of such breaches and submit the log to HHS annually no later than 60 days after the end of each calendar year.

### Designated Contact

The Privacy Officer is responsible for all HHS notifications and shall retain documentation of all submissions.

title: Breach Log and Tracking Policy
description: Policy for maintaining a log of all PHI breaches and near-misses.
bodyMarkdown:
## Breach Log and Tracking Policy

**Purpose:** To maintain a comprehensive log of all PHI breaches discovered at [PRACTICE NAME] for reporting and quality improvement purposes.

### Log Contents

For each discovered breach, the log shall record: date of discovery, date of occurrence (if known), description, number and type of individuals affected, PHI involved, risk assessment outcome, notifications sent, and corrective actions taken.

### Near-Miss Tracking

Incidents that were assessed and determined NOT to be reportable breaches shall also be logged with the risk assessment documentation supporting the determination.

### Retention

The breach log shall be retained for a minimum of 6 years and reviewed quarterly by the Privacy Officer.

title: Unsecured PHI Definition and Safeguards Policy
description: Defines unsecured PHI and the safeguards required to render PHI unusable, unreadable, or indecipherable.
bodyMarkdown:
## Unsecured PHI Definition and Safeguards Policy

**Purpose:** To define "unsecured PHI" and implement the HHS-specified safeguards that render PHI unusable, unreadable, or indecipherable to unauthorized persons, thereby triggering breach notification exemptions under 45 CFR §164.402.

### Definition

"Unsecured PHI" is PHI that has not been rendered unusable, unreadable, or indecipherable through encryption or destruction per HHS guidance.

### Approved Safeguards

- Encryption: NIST-approved encryption (AES 128-bit or higher) renders ePHI secured

- Destruction: Paper PHI shredded to HHS standards; electronic media degaussed or destroyed

### Practical Implication

Properly encrypted ePHI that is lost or stolen does not trigger breach notification requirements. All portable devices and media containing ePHI must be encrypted.

title: Exposure Control Plan
description: OSHA-required written plan to eliminate or minimize occupational exposure to bloodborne pathogens.
bodyMarkdown:
## Bloodborne Pathogen Exposure Control Plan

**Purpose:** To eliminate or minimize occupational exposure to bloodborne pathogens in compliance with OSHA's Bloodborne Pathogen Standard (29 CFR 1910.1030).

### Exposure Determination

[PRACTICE NAME] has determined that the following job classifications involve occupational exposure to blood or OPIM (other potentially infectious materials): [list clinical roles]. The following tasks involve exposure: phlebotomy, wound care, injection administration, handling contaminated instruments.

### Control Methods

- Universal Precautions: Treat all blood and OPIM as potentially infectious

- Engineering Controls: Use of sharps containers, needleless systems, self-sheathing needles

- Work Practice Controls: Prohibit recapping needles by hand; wash hands after glove removal

- PPE: Gloves, gowns, masks, eye protection as appropriate to task

### Plan Review

This plan shall be reviewed and updated annually and whenever new procedures or tasks create new exposure risks.

title: Post-Exposure Evaluation and Follow-Up Policy
description: Procedures for managing employee occupational exposures to bloodborne pathogens.
bodyMarkdown:
## Post-Exposure Evaluation and Follow-Up Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for evaluating and following up occupational exposures to bloodborne pathogens per 29 CFR 1910.1030(f).

### Immediate Steps Following Exposure

1. Wash needlestick or cut with soap and water; flush splashes to nose, mouth, or skin with water

2. Flush eyes with clean water or saline

3. Report exposure immediately to supervisor/manager

4. Seek medical evaluation as soon as possible (within 2 hours for highest-risk exposures)

### Medical Evaluation

[PRACTICE NAME] shall provide a confidential medical evaluation at no cost to the employee, including: documentation, source patient testing (if consent obtained), HBV, HCV, HIV testing, counseling, and prophylaxis as medically indicated.

### Confidentiality

All medical information related to exposure follow-up is confidential and shall not be shared with supervisors or other staff.

title: Sharps Injury Log Policy
description: Policy for recording and maintaining the OSHA-required sharps injury log.
bodyMarkdown:
## Sharps Injury Log Policy

**Purpose:** To maintain a sharps injury log in compliance with OSHA's requirement under 29 CFR 1904.8 and the Needlestick Safety and Prevention Act.

### Required Log Contents

For each recorded sharps injury: date of injury, type and brand of device involved, department or work area, explanation of how the incident occurred.

### Confidentiality

The log shall be maintained in a way that protects the confidentiality of the injured employee. Identifying information shall be removed before sharing.

### Retention and Review

The log shall be retained for at least 5 years. The log shall be reviewed annually to identify patterns and evaluate the effectiveness of prevention measures.

title: Hepatitis B Vaccination Program Policy
description: Policy for offering and tracking Hepatitis B vaccinations for at-risk employees.
bodyMarkdown:
## Hepatitis B Vaccination Program Policy

**Purpose:** To ensure [PRACTICE NAME] offers Hepatitis B vaccination to all employees with occupational exposure to bloodborne pathogens per 29 CFR 1910.1030(f)(2).

### Vaccine Offer

Hepatitis B vaccination shall be made available at no cost to all employees identified as having occupational exposure, within 10 working days of initial assignment.

### Declining Vaccination

Employees who decline the vaccine must sign a declination form. Employees may request the vaccine at a later date.

### Post-Vaccination Testing

Post-vaccination titer testing shall be offered 1–2 months after completing the vaccination series to verify immune response.

### Documentation

All vaccination records and declinations shall be maintained in the employee's confidential medical record.

title: Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) Selection and Use Policy
description: Policy for selecting, using, and maintaining PPE to protect against bloodborne pathogens.
bodyMarkdown:
## PPE Selection and Use Policy — Bloodborne Pathogens

**Purpose:** To ensure appropriate PPE is available, used, and maintained to protect employees from bloodborne pathogen exposure per 29 CFR 1910.1030(d)(3).

### PPE Selection

- Gloves: Required for any anticipated contact with blood, OPIM, mucous membranes, or non-intact skin

- Gowns/Aprons: Required when clothing may be contaminated with blood or OPIM

- Masks + Eye Protection: Required when splashes or sprays of blood or OPIM are reasonably anticipated

- Face Shields: Required during procedures with high splash risk

### Provision and Cost

[PRACTICE NAME] shall provide appropriate PPE at no cost to employees. PPE must be accessible in all clinical areas.

### Removal and Disposal

PPE shall be removed before leaving the work area and placed in designated containers for washing or disposal.

title: Housekeeping and Decontamination Policy
description: Policy for maintaining a clean work environment and decontaminating surfaces exposed to bloodborne pathogens.
bodyMarkdown:
## Housekeeping and Decontamination Policy

**Purpose:** To maintain a clean and sanitary work environment and establish procedures for decontaminating work surfaces per 29 CFR 1910.1030(d)(4).

### Cleaning Schedule

All clinical work surfaces shall be cleaned and decontaminated after completion of procedures, when surfaces are contaminated with blood or OPIM, and at the end of each work shift.

### Approved Disinfectants

Use EPA-registered hospital-grade disinfectants appropriate for bloodborne pathogen decontamination. Follow product label contact time requirements.

### Contaminated Sharps

Sharps containers must be replaced when 3/4 full. Overfilling is prohibited. Containers must be leak-proof, puncture-resistant, and properly labeled with the biohazard symbol.

title: Biohazard Labels and Signs Policy
description: Policy for using required biohazard warning labels on containers, equipment, and areas.
bodyMarkdown:
## Biohazard Labels and Signs Policy

**Purpose:** To ensure appropriate biohazard warning labels are used throughout [PRACTICE NAME] per 29 CFR 1910.1030(g)(1).

### Required Labeling

The biohazard symbol (fluorescent orange or orange-red) must be affixed to: containers of regulated waste, refrigerators/freezers containing blood or OPIM, containers used to store or transport blood or OPIM, contaminated equipment pending service.

### Exceptions

Red bags or red containers may be substituted for biohazard labels. Individual containers within a larger labeled container do not need individual labels.

### Placement

Labels must be located on the container, be clearly visible, and remain attached during storage, handling, and transport.

title: Bloodborne Pathogen Training Policy
description: Policy for annual bloodborne pathogen training for all at-risk employees.
bodyMarkdown:
## Bloodborne Pathogen Training Policy

**Purpose:** To ensure all employees with occupational exposure receive adequate bloodborne pathogen training per 29 CFR 1910.1030(g)(2).

### Timing

Training must be provided: (1) at the time of initial assignment to tasks with occupational exposure, and (2) annually thereafter.

### Training Content

Training must include: the epidemiology of bloodborne diseases; modes of transmission; the Exposure Control Plan; engineering and work practice controls; PPE selection and use; Hepatitis B vaccine program; exposure incident response; post-exposure evaluation; and signs, labels, and color-coding.

### Documentation

Training records must be retained for 3 years and include: dates, content summary, trainer name/qualifications, and names/job titles of attendees.

title: Hazard Communication Program
description: Written HazCom program meeting OSHA's Hazard Communication Standard (HCS 2012).
bodyMarkdown:
## Hazard Communication Program

**Purpose:** To ensure employees are informed about hazardous chemicals in the workplace per OSHA's Hazard Communication Standard (29 CFR 1910.1200).

### Labels

[PRACTICE NAME] shall ensure all containers of hazardous chemicals are labeled with: product identifier, signal word, hazard statements, precautionary statements, pictograms, and supplier information per GHS requirements.

### Safety Data Sheets (SDS)

An SDS shall be maintained for every hazardous chemical used at [PRACTICE NAME]. SDS are accessible to all employees during each work shift in [LOCATION].

### Employee Training

All employees shall receive HazCom training at initial hire and when new hazards are introduced. Training shall cover: how to read labels and SDS, physical and health hazards, protective measures, and emergency procedures.

### Chemical Inventory

A comprehensive chemical inventory shall be maintained and updated at least annually.

title: Safety Data Sheet (SDS) Management Policy
description: Policy for obtaining, maintaining, and accessing Safety Data Sheets for all hazardous chemicals.
bodyMarkdown:
## Safety Data Sheet (SDS) Management Policy

**Purpose:** To ensure Safety Data Sheets are obtained, maintained, and accessible for all hazardous chemicals per 29 CFR 1910.1200(g).

### Obtaining SDS

Before a new chemical is used, the supervisor shall obtain the SDS from the manufacturer or distributor. If an SDS is not provided, it must be requested before use of the chemical.

### SDS Location

SDS are maintained in [LOCATION/SYSTEM]. Electronic SDS systems must be accessible at all times, including during power outages (hard copy backup required).

### Review

SDS shall be reviewed annually and updated with current versions. Outdated SDS shall be archived but not discarded (retain for 30 years per OSHA records retention requirements).

title: Chemical Inventory Policy
description: Policy for maintaining a current inventory of all hazardous chemicals at the practice.
bodyMarkdown:
## Chemical Inventory Policy

**Purpose:** To maintain an accurate and current list of all hazardous chemicals present in [PRACTICE NAME]'s workplace per 29 CFR 1910.1200(e).

### Inventory Contents

The chemical inventory shall include: chemical name, common name, product identifier, location used/stored, and reference to the corresponding SDS.

### Update Frequency

The inventory shall be updated: (1) when a new chemical is introduced, (2) when a chemical is permanently removed, and (3) at a minimum annually.

### Responsibility

[DESIGNATED PERSON/ROLE] is responsible for maintaining the chemical inventory. A copy shall be kept with the Hazard Communication Program binder.

title: Chemical Container Labeling Policy
description: Policy for ensuring all hazardous chemical containers are properly labeled.
bodyMarkdown:
## Chemical Container Labeling Policy

**Purpose:** To ensure all containers of hazardous chemicals are properly labeled per GHS requirements under 29 CFR 1910.1200(f).

### Original Container Labels

Labels on original containers from manufacturers must not be defaced or removed. Damaged or illegible labels must be replaced immediately.

### Secondary Container Labels

When chemicals are transferred to secondary containers, the container must be labeled with: product identifier, hazard pictograms, signal word, hazard statements, and precautionary statements.

### Workplace Labels

Workplace labels must be legible, in English, and prominently displayed. [PRACTICE NAME] may use alternative labeling systems (e.g., HMIS, NFPA) in addition to GHS labels as long as GHS requirements are met.

title: HazCom Employee Training Policy
description: Policy for training employees on chemical hazards in the workplace.
bodyMarkdown:
## HazCom Employee Training Policy

**Purpose:** To ensure all employees receive training on hazardous chemicals in their work area per 29 CFR 1910.1200(h).

### Training Timing

Training shall be provided: (1) at time of hire before working with any hazardous chemicals, and (2) when new hazards are introduced into the work area.

### Training Content

- Location and availability of the written HazCom program and chemical inventory

- How to read and understand GHS labels and SDS

- Physical and health hazards of chemicals in the work area

- Protective measures (engineering controls, PPE, work practices)

- Emergency procedures for chemical spills or exposures

### Documentation

Training records shall include date, content covered, trainer name, and employee names. Retain for duration of employment plus 30 years.

title: Chemical Spill Response Policy
description: Procedures for responding to chemical spills to protect employee and patient safety.
bodyMarkdown:
## Chemical Spill Response Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for responding to chemical spills at [PRACTICE NAME] to ensure employee and patient safety.

### Immediate Response

1. Alert others in the area of the spill

2. Evacuate patients and non-essential personnel from the area

3. Do not attempt to clean up a spill without appropriate training and PPE

4. Consult the SDS for the specific chemical for spill response instructions

### Spill Cleanup

Only trained employees with appropriate PPE shall clean up chemical spills. Spill cleanup materials shall be disposed of as hazardous waste if required.

### Large or Uncontrolled Spills

For large spills or releases of highly toxic chemicals, evacuate the building and call 911. Do not re-enter until cleared by emergency responders.

### Reporting

All chemical spills must be documented in the incident log.

title: Emergency Action Plan
description: OSHA-required written emergency action plan for workplace emergencies.
bodyMarkdown:
## Emergency Action Plan

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for employee and patient safety during workplace emergencies per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.38.

### Emergency Reporting

For life-threatening emergencies, call 911 immediately. For internal reporting, contact [EMERGENCY CONTACT/ROLE] at [PHONE NUMBER].

### Evacuation Procedures

Upon activation of the fire alarm or direction to evacuate: (1) assist patients requiring help, (2) exit via nearest safe exit, (3) proceed to designated assembly area at [LOCATION], (4) do not use elevators, (5) do not re-enter until given all-clear.

### Employee Roles

- Emergency Coordinator: [ROLE] — oversees evacuation, contacts emergency services

- Floor Wardens: [ROLE] — sweep their area to ensure all staff/patients evacuate

- Accounting for Personnel: Supervisors account for all employees at assembly area

### Training

All employees shall be trained on the EAP at hire and annually. The plan shall be reviewed and updated annually.

title: Fire Prevention Plan
description: Written fire prevention plan identifying fire hazards and prevention procedures.
bodyMarkdown:
## Fire Prevention Plan

**Purpose:** To identify potential fire hazards and establish procedures to prevent fires per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.39.

### Potential Fire Hazards

- Electrical equipment and overloaded circuits

- Flammable chemicals and cleaning products

- Oxygen storage and use in clinical areas

- Combustible waste materials

- Improperly stored paper and cardboard

### Housekeeping Procedures

Combustible materials shall not be stored near heat sources. Waste materials shall be removed daily. Storage areas shall be kept clean and organized.

### Maintenance

Fire extinguishers shall be inspected monthly and serviced annually. Smoke detectors shall be tested quarterly. Sprinkler systems shall be inspected per manufacturer and code requirements.

title: Evacuation Routes and Procedures Policy
description: Policy for establishing and communicating evacuation routes throughout the facility.
bodyMarkdown:
## Evacuation Routes and Procedures Policy

**Purpose:** To establish clearly marked evacuation routes and procedures to ensure safe and orderly evacuation during emergencies.

### Route Designation

Primary and secondary evacuation routes have been designated for each area of [PRACTICE NAME]. Routes are posted on evacuation maps located in [LOCATIONS].

### Assembly Areas

All evacuation routes lead to designated outdoor assembly areas. The primary assembly area is [LOCATION]. The secondary assembly area is [LOCATION].

### Patients with Mobility Limitations

Staff shall assist patients who require help evacuating. Each clinical area shall designate a staff member responsible for assisting patients with mobility limitations.

### Map Maintenance

Evacuation maps shall be updated when the facility layout changes and reviewed annually.

title: Emergency Contact and Communication Policy
description: Policy for maintaining emergency contacts and communication procedures.
bodyMarkdown:
## Emergency Contact and Communication Policy

**Purpose:** To ensure [PRACTICE NAME] maintains current emergency contacts and procedures for notifying relevant parties during emergencies.

### Emergency Contact List

An up-to-date emergency contact list shall be maintained including: local police, fire, and EMS (911); poison control (1-800-222-1222); utility companies; building management; practice owner/administrator; and IT/EHR vendor emergency line.

### Employee Emergency Contacts

Each employee shall provide an emergency contact at hire and update as needed. Emergency contact information is kept confidential and used only in genuine emergencies.

### Communication During Emergencies

During emergencies, the Emergency Coordinator is responsible for communications. Staff shall not post about ongoing emergencies on social media.

title: Shelter-in-Place Policy
description: Procedures for sheltering in place during external emergencies such as severe weather or hazmat incidents.
bodyMarkdown:
## Shelter-in-Place Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for sheltering in place during external emergencies including severe weather, active threat situations, or hazmat incidents.

### When to Shelter in Place

Shelter-in-place is appropriate when: hazardous materials release occurs outside; law enforcement directs sheltering; severe weather (tornado warning) is issued; or active threat is in the vicinity.

### Procedures

1. Move patients and staff to the designated shelter area: [LOCATION]

2. Close and lock all exterior doors and windows

3. Turn off HVAC systems (for hazmat events)

4. Monitor emergency broadcasts for all-clear

5. Await guidance from emergency services before resuming operations

### Communication

Activate shelter-in-place by announcing over intercom or using the code word "[CODE WORD]". Do not evacuate patients unless directed by emergency services.

title: Injury and Illness Prevention Program (IIPP)
description: Core workplace safety program for identifying and correcting workplace hazards.
bodyMarkdown:
## Injury and Illness Prevention Program (IIPP)

**Purpose:** To establish a comprehensive program for identifying, evaluating, and correcting workplace safety hazards at [PRACTICE NAME].

### Management Commitment

[PRACTICE NAME] is committed to providing a safe and healthy work environment. Safety responsibilities are assigned to [ROLE/TITLE].

### Hazard Identification and Correction

Workplace safety inspections shall be conducted monthly. Employees are encouraged to report hazards without fear of retaliation. Identified hazards shall be corrected promptly or controls implemented to protect employees while corrections are made.

### Employee Training

All employees receive safety training at hire and annually. Specific hazard training is provided when new hazards are introduced.

### Recordkeeping

Injuries and illnesses are recorded on OSHA 300 logs as required. All safety inspections, trainings, and corrective actions are documented.

title: Slip, Trip, and Fall Prevention Policy
description: Policy for identifying and controlling slip, trip, and fall hazards.
bodyMarkdown:
## Slip, Trip, and Fall Prevention Policy

**Purpose:** To identify and eliminate slip, trip, and fall hazards at [PRACTICE NAME] to protect employees and patients.

### Floor and Aisle Maintenance

Floors shall be kept clean, dry, and free of clutter. Spills shall be cleaned up immediately and warning cones placed until the area is dry. Non-slip mats shall be placed at entrances and in areas prone to moisture.

### Lighting

All work areas shall be adequately lit. Burned-out lights shall be replaced within 24 hours of discovery.

### Reporting

All slip, trip, and fall incidents (including near-misses) shall be reported to the supervisor immediately and documented. Near-misses are reviewed to prevent future incidents.

title: Ergonomics and Musculoskeletal Disorder Prevention Policy
description: Policy for reducing ergonomic risk factors and preventing musculoskeletal injuries.
bodyMarkdown:
## Ergonomics and Musculoskeletal Disorder Prevention Policy

**Purpose:** To identify and reduce ergonomic risk factors that may lead to musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs) among employees at [PRACTICE NAME].

### Ergonomic Risk Factors

Common ergonomic hazards include: repetitive motions, awkward postures, forceful exertions, prolonged static positions, and contact stress. These are present in healthcare settings during patient handling, computer work, and laboratory tasks.

### Controls

- Adjustable workstations for computer users

- Patient lift equipment and transfer aids

- Team lifting for patients over [WEIGHT] lbs

- Job rotation to reduce repetitive motion exposure

- Regular breaks from static postures

### Reporting

Employees experiencing ergonomic discomfort or MSD symptoms shall report to their supervisor. Early intervention resources and medical evaluation shall be offered.

title: Electrical Safety Policy
description: Policy for the safe use of electrical equipment and prevention of electrical hazards.
bodyMarkdown:
## Electrical Safety Policy

**Purpose:** To protect employees from electrical hazards in the workplace per applicable OSHA standards.

### General Rules

- Never use damaged or frayed electrical cords

- Do not overload electrical outlets or extension cords

- Keep liquids away from all electrical equipment

- Unplug equipment before performing maintenance or cleaning

- Do not use extension cords as permanent wiring

### Medical Equipment

All electrical medical equipment must be inspected and certified by a biomedical engineer before use. Defective medical equipment must be immediately taken out of service and tagged.

### Reporting

Report all electrical hazards, sparking, or burning smells immediately to the facilities manager or supervisor.

title: General Housekeeping and Facility Safety Policy
description: Policy for maintaining a clean, safe, and organized workplace.
bodyMarkdown:
## General Housekeeping and Facility Safety Policy

**Purpose:** To maintain a clean, safe, and organized workplace at [PRACTICE NAME] to prevent accidents and maintain a professional environment for patients and staff.

### Daily Requirements

- Keep all aisles and exits clear of obstructions at all times

- Clean and sanitize work areas at the end of each shift

- Dispose of waste in designated containers

- Return equipment and supplies to designated storage locations

### Storage

Heavy items shall be stored at waist height or lower. Lighter items may be stored higher. Shelving shall not be overloaded beyond rated capacity.

### Inspections

Supervisors shall conduct weekly housekeeping inspections. Deficiencies shall be corrected within 24 hours or sooner if presenting an immediate hazard.

title: Workplace Violence Prevention Policy
description: Policy for preventing and responding to workplace violence including patient aggression.
bodyMarkdown:
## Workplace Violence Prevention Policy

**Purpose:** To establish measures to prevent and respond to workplace violence at [PRACTICE NAME] in accordance with OSHA's General Duty Clause.

### Zero Tolerance

[PRACTICE NAME] has zero tolerance for violent behavior by patients, visitors, or staff. All incidents of actual or threatened violence shall be reported and investigated.

### Prevention Measures

- Controlled access to clinical areas

- Training for de-escalation of agitated patients

- Buddy system for late-night or isolated work areas

- Panic button / emergency alert system in high-risk areas

### Reporting and Response

All violent incidents or threats must be reported to the supervisor and documented. For immediate threats, call 911. Affected employees shall be offered post-incident counseling.

title: Latex Allergy Prevention Policy
description: Policy for protecting employees and patients with latex sensitivity from exposure.
bodyMarkdown:
## Latex Allergy Prevention Policy

**Purpose:** To protect employees and patients with latex sensitivity or allergy from latex exposure at [PRACTICE NAME].

### Identification

All patients shall be screened for latex allergy or sensitivity during intake. Latex-allergic patients shall be flagged in the medical record and scheduled as the first procedure of the day.

### Latex-Safe Environment

When a latex-allergic patient is present: use only powder-free, latex-free gloves; remove all latex-containing products from the area; prepare a latex-free supply cart.

### Employee Screening

Employees with suspected latex sensitivity shall be referred for medical evaluation. Latex-free alternatives shall be provided upon request or medical recommendation.

title: Back Safety and Safe Patient Handling Policy
description: Policy for preventing back injuries through safe patient handling and body mechanics.
bodyMarkdown:
## Back Safety and Safe Patient Handling Policy

**Purpose:** To prevent back injuries among healthcare workers through safe patient handling practices and proper body mechanics training.

### Manual Lift Restrictions

Manual patient lifting is prohibited except in life-threatening emergencies. Patient handling equipment (lift devices, transfer belts, slide sheets) shall be used for all patient repositioning, transfers, and ambulation assistance.

### Body Mechanics

Employees shall be trained in proper body mechanics: maintain neutral spine, bend at knees not waist, keep loads close to body, avoid twisting while carrying, get help for heavy or awkward loads.

### Equipment Availability

Patient handling equipment shall be available in all clinical areas. Staff shall be trained on proper use of all available equipment.

title: Code of Conduct
description: Organizational code of conduct governing employee behavior and ethical standards.
bodyMarkdown:
## Code of Conduct

[PRACTICE NAME] is committed to conducting its business with the highest ethical standards. This Code of Conduct applies to all workforce members, contractors, and volunteers.

### Core Principles

- Integrity: Be honest and transparent in all interactions

- Respect: Treat all patients, colleagues, and visitors with dignity

- Accountability: Take responsibility for your actions

- Confidentiality: Protect patient and business information

- Compliance: Follow all applicable laws, regulations, and policies

### Conflicts of Interest

Workforce members must disclose any personal or financial interest that could conflict with [PRACTICE NAME]'s interests. Gifts from vendors or patients exceeding $[VALUE] must be reported.

### Reporting Violations

Suspected violations of this Code shall be reported to [CONTACT/HOTLINE]. Reports may be made anonymously without fear of retaliation.

title: Whistleblower Protection Policy
description: Policy protecting employees who report compliance concerns from retaliation.
bodyMarkdown:
## Whistleblower Protection Policy

**Purpose:** To protect employees who report compliance concerns, fraud, or illegal activity from retaliation.

### Protected Activity

Employees are protected when they: report suspected violations of law or [PRACTICE NAME] policy; file a complaint with a regulatory agency; participate in an investigation; refuse to participate in illegal activity.

### Non-Retaliation

[PRACTICE NAME] prohibits retaliation against any employee who engages in protected activity. Retaliation includes: termination, demotion, reduction in pay, harassment, or any adverse employment action.

### Reporting Procedure

Concerns may be reported to: immediate supervisor, practice administrator, HR, or anonymously through [HOTLINE/EMAIL]. All reports shall be investigated promptly and confidentially.

title: Records Retention Policy
description: Policy establishing retention periods for medical, business, and compliance records.
bodyMarkdown:
## Records Retention Policy

**Purpose:** To establish minimum retention periods for medical and business records at [PRACTICE NAME] in accordance with applicable federal and [STATE] state law.

### Retention Schedule

- Medical Records (Adults): 10 years from last treatment, or as required by state law

- Medical Records (Minors): Until age of majority + 3 years, or 10 years, whichever is longer

- HIPAA Privacy and Security Policies: 6 years from creation or last effective date

- HIPAA Authorization Forms: 6 years

- OSHA Records (300 Log): 5 years following the calendar year covered

- Employee Medical Records: Duration of employment + 30 years (OSHA)

- Billing Records: 7 years

### Destruction

Records shall be destroyed securely (shredding for paper, secure erasure for electronic) at the end of their retention period. A destruction log shall be maintained.

title: Social Media Policy
description: Policy governing employee use of social media as it relates to patients and the practice.
bodyMarkdown:
## Social Media Policy

**Purpose:** To establish guidelines for employee use of social media to protect patient privacy, practice reputation, and HIPAA compliance.

### Absolute Prohibitions

- Never post any patient information, photos, or stories — even if seemingly de-identified

- Never post photos taken inside [PRACTICE NAME] that could reveal patient presence

- Never post confidential business information, financial data, or personnel matters

- Never make derogatory comments about patients, colleagues, or [PRACTICE NAME]

### Personal Social Media

Employees are responsible for ensuring their personal social media activity does not violate HIPAA or embarrass [PRACTICE NAME]. Identify yourself as an employee only if doing so is relevant and truthful, and make clear opinions are your own.

### Violations

Social media violations of HIPAA are subject to the full range of sanctions under the Sanction Policy, up to and including termination and referral to OCR.

title: Telehealth Policy
description: Policy governing telehealth service delivery and associated HIPAA requirements.
bodyMarkdown:
## Telehealth Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for delivering telehealth services at [PRACTICE NAME] in compliance with HIPAA and applicable state telehealth regulations.

### Approved Platforms

Only HIPAA-compliant telehealth platforms with signed BAAs shall be used for clinical consultations. Platforms: [PLATFORM NAME(S)]. Consumer video tools (FaceTime, Zoom free, Skype) are not permitted without an executed BAA.

### Patient Consent

Informed consent for telehealth must be obtained before the first telehealth visit and documented in the medical record.

### Privacy During Sessions

Providers must conduct telehealth visits in a private location. Patients shall be reminded to use a private location. Headsets are recommended to prevent audio from being overheard.

### Documentation

Telehealth encounters shall be documented in the EHR the same as in-person visits, including the patient's location at time of service.

title: Mobile Device and BYOD Policy
description: Policy for use of mobile devices — both practice-owned and personal — to access PHI.
bodyMarkdown:
## Mobile Device and BYOD Policy

**Purpose:** To establish security requirements for mobile devices used to access [PRACTICE NAME]'s systems or PHI.

### Practice-Owned Devices

- Must be encrypted (AES-256 or device-level encryption)

- Must have remote wipe capability enrolled before use

- Must use strong PIN or biometric authentication

- Automatic screen lock after 5 minutes of inactivity

### Personal Devices (BYOD)

Personal devices may access [PRACTICE NAME] systems only through the approved Mobile Device Management (MDM) solution or through web-only portal access. Personal devices must meet the same security requirements as practice-owned devices.

### Lost or Stolen Devices

Lost or stolen devices must be reported immediately to the Security Officer for remote wipe. This event must be assessed as a potential breach.

title: Password and Credential Management Policy
description: Policy establishing password requirements and credential management standards.
bodyMarkdown:
## Password and Credential Management Policy

**Purpose:** To establish requirements for creating and managing passwords and credentials for all systems at [PRACTICE NAME].

### Password Requirements

- Minimum 12 characters

- Must include: uppercase letter, lowercase letter, number, special character

- Must be changed every 90 days

- Cannot reuse the last 10 passwords

- Must not include dictionary words, usernames, or personal information

### Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)

MFA is required for: all remote access, all cloud-based applications, email access from outside the office network, and administrative system access.

### Password Storage

Passwords shall never be shared, written on paper, or stored in unsecured documents. Approved password managers may be used. Vendor/shared credentials must be stored in a secure credential vault.

title: Remote Work Policy
description: Policy for employees working remotely, including security requirements for home offices.
bodyMarkdown:
## Remote Work Policy

**Purpose:** To establish requirements for employees working remotely to maintain security and HIPAA compliance.

### Eligibility

Remote work arrangements require prior written approval from [SUPERVISOR/HR]. Not all positions are eligible for remote work.

### Security Requirements

- Use only [PRACTICE NAME]-approved devices for accessing clinical systems

- Connect via VPN when accessing internal systems or PHI

- Home network must be password-protected (WPA2 or higher)

- Work in a private area where PHI cannot be overheard or seen by others

- Lock screen immediately when stepping away

- Do not print PHI at home

### Monitoring

[PRACTICE NAME] reserves the right to monitor activity on practice-owned devices and systems, including remote sessions.

title: Vendor Management Policy
description: Policy for evaluating, contracting with, and overseeing vendors and service providers.
bodyMarkdown:
## Vendor Management Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for evaluating and managing third-party vendors and service providers, particularly those with access to PHI.

### Vendor Assessment

Before engaging a new vendor that may have access to PHI, the Privacy/Security Officer shall assess: security practices, breach history, subcontractor management, and data handling procedures.

### BAA Requirement

A signed BAA must be in place before any vendor can access, receive, maintain, or transmit PHI. This requirement applies to subcontractors handling PHI on behalf of [PRACTICE NAME].

### Ongoing Monitoring

Vendor relationships shall be reviewed annually. BAAs shall be updated when material changes occur. Vendors experiencing a breach affecting [PRACTICE NAME] must notify [PRACTICE NAME] promptly per the BAA.

title: Conflict of Interest Policy
description: Policy for identifying and managing conflicts of interest among workforce members.
bodyMarkdown:
## Conflict of Interest Policy

**Purpose:** To identify and manage conflicts of interest that could impair objective decision-making at [PRACTICE NAME].

### Definition

A conflict of interest exists when an employee's personal, financial, or other interests could improperly influence their professional judgment or actions on behalf of [PRACTICE NAME].

### Disclosure Requirements

Employees must disclose any potential conflict of interest to their supervisor or administrator, including: financial interests in competing businesses, gifts or payments from vendors, personal relationships affecting business decisions.

### Resolution

Disclosed conflicts shall be reviewed by [DESIGNATED PERSON/COMMITTEE]. Resolution options include recusal from relevant decisions, divestiture, or other appropriate action.

title: Employee Handbook Acknowledgment
description: Acknowledgment form for new employees confirming receipt and review of the employee handbook.
bodyMarkdown:
## Employee Handbook Acknowledgment

I, [EMPLOYEE NAME], acknowledge that I have received, read, and understand the [PRACTICE NAME] Employee Handbook. I understand that the handbook contains important information about [PRACTICE NAME]'s policies, procedures, and expectations.

### Key Acknowledgments

- I understand that the Employee Handbook is not a contract of employment

- I understand that [PRACTICE NAME] may modify policies at any time with appropriate notice

- I agree to comply with all policies and procedures outlined in the handbook

- I understand the consequences of violating practice policies, up to and including termination

### Questions

I have had the opportunity to ask questions about any policies I do not understand. For future questions, I will contact [HR/SUPERVISOR].

Employee Signature: _________________ Date: _________________

title: HIPAA Awareness Acknowledgment for New Employees
description: New employee acknowledgment of HIPAA training and obligations.
bodyMarkdown:
## HIPAA Awareness Acknowledgment for New Employees

I, [EMPLOYEE NAME], acknowledge that I have received HIPAA training as part of my orientation at [PRACTICE NAME] and that I understand my obligations under the HIPAA Privacy and Security Rules.

### I Understand and Agree To:

- Access only the minimum amount of PHI necessary to perform my job duties

- Never share patient information with anyone who does not have a clinical or administrative need to know

- Use only [PRACTICE NAME]-approved methods for transmitting PHI

- Report suspected privacy or security violations to the Privacy/Security Officer immediately

- Participate in annual HIPAA training as required

- Understand that violations may result in disciplinary action, termination, and personal civil/criminal liability

Employee Signature: _________________ Date: _________________

title: OSHA Orientation Acknowledgment for New Employees
description: New employee acknowledgment of OSHA safety orientation completion.
bodyMarkdown:
## OSHA Orientation Acknowledgment for New Employees

I, [EMPLOYEE NAME], acknowledge that I have received workplace safety orientation training at [PRACTICE NAME] covering OSHA requirements applicable to my role.

### Topics Covered:

- Emergency Action Plan and evacuation procedures

- Bloodborne pathogen exposure prevention and response

- Hazard Communication / Right-to-Know training

- Location of Safety Data Sheets

- Personal Protective Equipment available and required for my role

- How to report a work-related injury or illness

- Employee rights under OSHA

I understand that failure to follow safety policies may result in disciplinary action.

Employee Signature: _________________ Date: _________________

title: IT Acceptable Use Policy Acknowledgment
description: New employee acknowledgment of acceptable use policy for IT systems and devices.
bodyMarkdown:
## IT Acceptable Use Policy Acknowledgment

I, [EMPLOYEE NAME], acknowledge that I have reviewed [PRACTICE NAME]'s IT Acceptable Use Policy and agree to the following terms:

### Acceptable Uses

- Use of [PRACTICE NAME] IT systems is limited to legitimate work purposes

- I will use only my assigned user credentials and not share passwords

- I will report suspected security incidents or unauthorized access immediately

### Prohibited Uses

- Accessing, downloading, or distributing offensive, illegal, or inappropriate content

- Installing unauthorized software on practice-owned devices

- Circumventing security controls or accessing systems beyond my authorization

- Using practice systems for personal business or financial gain

### Monitoring

I understand that [PRACTICE NAME] may monitor use of its IT systems and that I have no expectation of privacy when using practice-owned devices or systems.

Employee Signature: _________________ Date: _________________

title: Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement
description: Confidentiality agreement for all workforce members protecting patient and business information.
bodyMarkdown:
## Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement

This Agreement is entered into between [PRACTICE NAME] ("Practice") and [EMPLOYEE NAME] ("Employee") as a condition of employment.

### Confidential Information

"Confidential Information" includes: all patient health information (PHI), financial and business records, personnel files, operational procedures, pricing, vendor agreements, and any other non-public business information.

### Employee Obligations

- Maintain the confidentiality of all Confidential Information during and after employment

- Use Confidential Information only for authorized work purposes

- Not disclose Confidential Information to unauthorized parties

- Report actual or suspected breaches of confidentiality immediately

### Duration

Obligations under this Agreement continue indefinitely after the termination of employment for PHI, and for [X] years for other Confidential Information.

Employee Signature: _________________ Date: _________________

title: Mental Health Records and Psychotherapy Notes Policy
description: Special protections for mental health records and psychotherapy notes under HIPAA.
bodyMarkdown:
## Mental Health Records and Psychotherapy Notes Policy

**Purpose:** To establish special protections for mental health records, particularly psychotherapy notes, which receive heightened protection under HIPAA and [STATE] law.

### Psychotherapy Notes Definition

Psychotherapy notes are notes recorded in any medium by a mental health professional documenting the contents of conversation during a private counseling session — separate from the rest of the medical record.

### Authorization Requirement

Psychotherapy notes may not be disclosed without a specific patient authorization, even for treatment, payment, or operations. The authorization must be separate from general authorizations.

### Storage

Psychotherapy notes shall be stored separately from the general medical record in a secure location with restricted access.

### State Law

This practice shall comply with all [STATE] state laws regarding mental health record confidentiality, which may be more stringent than HIPAA.

title: Substance Abuse Records Policy (42 CFR Part 2)
description: Policy for handling substance abuse treatment records under the more restrictive 42 CFR Part 2.
bodyMarkdown:
## Substance Abuse Records Policy (42 CFR Part 2)

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for handling substance use disorder (SUD) patient records in compliance with 42 CFR Part 2, which provides stronger protections than HIPAA.

### Application

This policy applies to records of patients treated for substance use disorders at programs that receive federal assistance, directly or indirectly.

### General Rule

SUD records may not be disclosed without: patient written consent; a court order; or a statutory exception (medical emergency, research, audit).

### Key Differences from HIPAA

- Treatment, payment, and operations disclosures require patient consent unless integrated care provisions apply

- Law enforcement access is more restricted than under HIPAA

- Records must include a notice that re-disclosure is prohibited

### Redisclosure Notice

All disclosed SUD records must include the required federal prohibition on redisclosure notice.

title: Dental Practice PHI Handling Policy
