description: HIPAA privacy policy tailored for dental practice records and radiographs.
bodyMarkdown:
## Dental Practice PHI Handling Policy

**Purpose:** To address the unique PHI considerations in a dental practice setting, including dental records, radiographs, and treatment charts.

### Dental Records as PHI

All dental records constitute PHI under HIPAA, including: treatment notes, dental charts, radiographs (digital and film), photographs, study models, and billing records.

### Radiograph Handling

Digital radiographs shall be stored encrypted. Film radiographs shall be stored in locked cabinets. Transfer of radiographs shall be via secure portal or encrypted email only.

### Patient Photographs

Intraoral and extraoral photographs are PHI. Patient consent (separate from treatment consent) is required for use of photographs in marketing, case studies, or staff training materials.

### Interoffice Communications

Communications with specialists, labs, and referring providers containing patient information must use HIPAA-compliant secure transmission methods.

title: Chiropractic Records and Imaging Policy
description: HIPAA policy for handling chiropractic patient records and diagnostic imaging.
bodyMarkdown:
## Chiropractic Records and Imaging Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for managing chiropractic patient records, x-rays, and related PHI in compliance with HIPAA and [STATE] chiropractic board requirements.

### Records Content

Chiropractic records shall include: patient intake forms, health history, examination findings, x-ray reports and images, SOAP notes, treatment plans, and informed consent documentation.

### X-Ray and Imaging

X-rays and diagnostic images are PHI. Digital images must be stored encrypted. Images shared with other providers must use secure, HIPAA-compliant transmission. Patients have the right to copies of their images.

### Retention

Chiropractic records shall be retained per [STATE] chiropractic board requirements and HIPAA minimums (whichever is longer).

title: Pediatric Informed Consent and Minor Records Policy
description: Policy for obtaining consent for minor patients and managing their records.
bodyMarkdown:
## Pediatric Informed Consent and Minor Records Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for obtaining consent for treatment of minor patients and managing their health records in compliance with HIPAA and [STATE] law.

### General Rule

For minors, the parent or legal guardian is typically the personal representative under HIPAA and has rights to the minor's health records.

### Exceptions — When the Minor Controls

Under [STATE] law, a minor may have the right to consent to and control access to records related to: reproductive health, contraception, sexually transmitted infections, mental health (above a specified age), substance abuse treatment.

### Divorced/Separated Parents

Both parents retain rights to medical records unless a court order specifies otherwise. [PRACTICE NAME] shall request documentation of custody arrangements when disputes arise.

title: Minor Confidentiality Policy
description: Policy protecting confidentiality of sensitive health services provided to minors.
bodyMarkdown:
## Minor Confidentiality Policy

**Purpose:** To protect the confidentiality of health services provided to minor patients when they have the legal right to consent without parental involvement under [STATE] law.

### Services Covered

In [STATE], minors may consent to and receive confidential services for: [list applicable services per state law, e.g., STI testing and treatment, contraception, mental health services, substance abuse treatment].

### Billing Considerations

When a minor has received confidential services, [PRACTICE NAME] shall take reasonable steps to ensure EOBs or billing communications do not inadvertently disclose these services to parents. This may include: direct billing to the minor, using separate billing addresses, or working with the insurer on confidential communications.

title: Reproductive Health Information Privacy Policy
description: Special privacy protections for reproductive health information.
bodyMarkdown:
## Reproductive Health Information Privacy Policy

**Purpose:** To establish enhanced protections for reproductive health information consistent with HIPAA Privacy Rule amendments effective 2024 (45 CFR §164.502(a)(5)(iii)) and [STATE] law.

### Prohibition on Reproductive Health Disclosures

Effective April 2024, [PRACTICE NAME] may not use or disclose PHI related to reproductive health care for: conducting investigations of individuals seeking or obtaining such care; imposing liability on individuals for seeking or obtaining such care; or identifying individuals for such purposes.

### Attestation Requirement

For certain disclosures, [PRACTICE NAME] must obtain an attestation that the PHI will not be used for prohibited purposes.

### Training

All workforce members who handle PHI shall receive training on these enhanced protections.

title: Genetic Information (GINA) Privacy Policy
description: Policy protecting genetic information under HIPAA and the Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act.
bodyMarkdown:
## Genetic Information (GINA) Privacy Policy

**Purpose:** To protect genetic information as a special category of PHI under HIPAA (as amended by GINA) and the Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act.

### Definition

Genetic information includes information about an individual's genetic tests, the genetic tests of family members, the manifestation of disease in family members, and requests for or receipt of genetic services.

### Prohibited Uses

Genetic information may NOT be used for underwriting purposes. Health plans are prohibited from: using genetic information to determine eligibility or premiums; requiring or requesting genetic testing; discriminating based on genetic information.

### Practice Obligations

[PRACTICE NAME] shall not use or disclose genetic information in ways that violate GINA. All workforce members handling genetic test results shall receive training on these special protections.

title: HIV/AIDS Confidentiality Policy
description: Policy providing heightened confidentiality protections for HIV/AIDS-related information.
bodyMarkdown:
## HIV/AIDS Confidentiality Policy

**Purpose:** To protect HIV/AIDS-related health information under HIPAA and [STATE] state law, which typically provides heightened protections beyond HIPAA.

### [STATE] Law Requirements

[STATE] law may require specific written authorization (separate from standard HIPAA authorization) for disclosure of HIV-related information. The specific requirements include: [state-specific provisions].

### HIPAA Minimum

At minimum, HIV/AIDS information shall be treated as any other PHI under HIPAA. [PRACTICE NAME] shall apply the more protective standard between HIPAA and [STATE] law.

### Workforce Training

All clinical staff shall receive training on the special confidentiality requirements for HIV/AIDS-related information. Violations are subject to both HIPAA and state law penalties.

title: Telemedicine Consent and Documentation Policy
description: Policy for obtaining informed consent and documenting telemedicine encounters.
bodyMarkdown:
## Telemedicine Informed Consent and Documentation Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for obtaining informed consent for telemedicine services and properly documenting telehealth encounters at [PRACTICE NAME].

### Informed Consent Requirements

Before the first telemedicine visit, patients shall be informed of and consent to: the nature of telemedicine and its limitations; technology requirements; privacy and security measures; the right to discontinue at any time; alternative in-person options.

### Consent Documentation

Telemedicine consent shall be documented in the EHR. A copy shall be provided to the patient.

### Documentation Standards

Telemedicine notes shall include: patient location (city/state) at time of service, provider location, technology platform used, patient's verbal or written consent confirmation, and clinical findings as with any in-person visit.

title: OSHA Posting Requirements Compliance Checklist
description: Checklist ensuring all required OSHA postings are displayed at the workplace.
bodyMarkdown:
## OSHA Posting Requirements Compliance Policy

**Purpose:** To ensure [PRACTICE NAME] maintains all required OSHA postings in compliance with federal and [STATE] requirements.

### Required Federal Postings

- OSHA "Job Safety and Health — It's the Law" poster (Form 3165)

- OSHA 300A Summary of Work-Related Injuries and Illnesses (posted Feb 1 – April 30)

- FMLA Notice (if 50+ employees)

- FLSA Minimum Wage Poster

- EEO "Know Your Rights" Poster

### Posting Location

All required postings must be displayed in a conspicuous location where employees can readily see them, typically the break room or main employee entrance.

### Annual Review

The office manager shall verify all required postings are current and displayed at least annually and following any regulatory updates.

title: OSHA Injury and Illness Recordkeeping Policy
description: Policy for maintaining OSHA 300 logs and reporting work-related injuries and illnesses.
bodyMarkdown:
## OSHA Injury and Illness Recordkeeping Policy

**Purpose:** To comply with OSHA's injury and illness recordkeeping requirements under 29 CFR Part 1904.

### Recordable Incidents

A work-related injury or illness is recordable if it results in: death, days away from work, restricted work or job transfer, medical treatment beyond first aid, loss of consciousness, or diagnosis of a significant injury by a healthcare professional.

### OSHA 300 Log

All recordable incidents shall be entered on the OSHA 300 Log within 7 calendar days of learning of the injury or illness. The log shall be maintained for 5 years.

### OSHA 300A Annual Summary

The 300A summary shall be certified by a company executive and posted from February 1 through April 30 each year.

### Severe Injury Reporting

Work-related fatalities must be reported to OSHA within 8 hours. Hospitalizations, amputations, or eye losses must be reported within 24 hours.

title: Patient Complaint and Grievance Policy
description: Policy for receiving, investigating, and resolving patient complaints and grievances.
bodyMarkdown:
## Patient Complaint and Grievance Policy

**Purpose:** To establish a process for patients to express concerns about their care or the operations of [PRACTICE NAME] and to ensure timely, fair resolution.

### Submitting a Complaint

Patients may submit complaints verbally or in writing to any staff member or directly to [CONTACT/PRIVACY OFFICER]. Written complaints may be submitted via [METHOD].

### Response Timeline

All complaints shall receive an initial acknowledgment within 3 business days. A written response addressing the complaint shall be provided within 30 days.

### Investigation

All complaints shall be reviewed and investigated. Findings and any corrective actions shall be documented.

### Non-Retaliation

[PRACTICE NAME] shall not retaliate against any patient for filing a complaint. Patients retain the right to file complaints with applicable regulatory agencies.

title: Acceptable Use of AI in Healthcare Policy
description: Governs the use of artificial intelligence tools in clinical and administrative workflows at [PRACTICE NAME].
bodyMarkdown:
## Acceptable Use of AI in Healthcare Policy

**Purpose:** To establish guidelines for the responsible use of artificial intelligence (AI) tools in clinical and administrative functions at [PRACTICE NAME].

### Scope

This policy applies to all workforce members who use AI-powered tools including clinical decision support, documentation assistants, chatbots, and administrative automation.

### General Principles

- AI tools must never be used as the sole basis for clinical decisions — physician judgment must always be exercised

- No PHI may be entered into AI tools that are not covered by a BAA

- AI-generated content (notes, letters, reports) must be reviewed by a qualified human before use

- All AI tools processing PHI must be included in the practice's Security Risk Assessment

### Approved Tools

[PRACTICE NAME] maintains a list of approved AI tools. Using unapproved AI tools with patient data is prohibited.

### Training

Staff must complete AI usage training before using any AI tool in their workflow.

title: Data Backup and Recovery Policy
description: Establishes requirements for regular backup, secure storage, and recovery of electronic health information.
bodyMarkdown:
## Data Backup and Recovery Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for creating and maintaining retrievable exact copies of ePHI as required by HIPAA Security Rule §164.308(a)(7)(ii)(A).

### Backup Frequency

- Full backups: Weekly (at minimum)

- Incremental backups: Daily

- Transaction logs: Continuously

### Backup Storage

Backups must be stored in a geographically separate location from the primary data center. All backup media must be encrypted using AES-256 or equivalent.

### Recovery Testing

[PRACTICE NAME] shall test data restoration procedures at least quarterly to verify backup integrity and recovery capability. Tests must be documented.

### Retention

Backup data shall be retained for a minimum of [RETENTION PERIOD] in accordance with federal and state record retention requirements.

title: Patient Portal Privacy and Security Policy
description: Defines privacy and security requirements for the practice's patient portal.
bodyMarkdown:
## Patient Portal Privacy and Security Policy

**Purpose:** To establish privacy and security safeguards for [PRACTICE NAME]'s patient portal in compliance with HIPAA requirements.

### Authentication

Patient portal access requires unique credentials with multi-factor authentication. Accounts are locked after [N] failed login attempts.

### Data Transmission

All data transmitted between the portal and users must use TLS 1.2+ encryption. Patients should be educated about secure access practices.

### Access Logging

All portal access and activity must be logged and retained for a minimum of 6 years. Logs shall include user identity, date/time, and actions performed.

### Patient Responsibilities

Patients are responsible for maintaining the confidentiality of their login credentials and must report suspected unauthorized access immediately.

title: Drug-Free Workplace Policy
description: Establishes the practice's commitment to a drug-free workplace per the Drug-Free Workplace Act.
bodyMarkdown:
## Drug-Free Workplace Policy

**Purpose:** To maintain a safe, productive work environment free from the influence of illegal drugs and alcohol, consistent with the Drug-Free Workplace Act of 1988.

### Prohibited Conduct

- The unlawful manufacture, distribution, dispensation, possession, or use of controlled substances in the workplace

- Reporting to work under the influence of illegal drugs or alcohol

- Using prescription medications that impair the ability to safely perform job duties without proper notification

### Employee Obligations

Employees must notify [PRACTICE NAME] of any criminal drug conviction for a violation occurring in the workplace within five (5) days of such conviction.

### Consequences

Violation of this policy may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination, and/or required participation in a substance abuse treatment program.

### Employee Assistance

[PRACTICE NAME] encourages employees to seek help for substance abuse and will provide information about available treatment programs and resources.

title: Anti-Harassment and Non-Discrimination Policy
description: Prohibits workplace harassment and discrimination in accordance with Title VII, ADA, and state laws.
bodyMarkdown:
## Anti-Harassment and Non-Discrimination Policy

**Purpose:** To maintain a work environment free from harassment and discrimination based on protected characteristics.

### Protected Categories

[PRACTICE NAME] prohibits discrimination and harassment based on race, color, religion, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity, national origin, age, disability, genetic information, veteran status, or any other characteristic protected by law.

### Prohibited Conduct

- Unwelcome verbal, physical, or visual conduct that creates a hostile work environment

- Quid pro quo harassment (conditioning employment benefits on submission to unwelcome conduct)

- Retaliation against individuals who report harassment or participate in investigations

### Reporting

Employees who experience or witness harassment should report it to [CONTACT PERSON/DEPARTMENT]. Reports may be made verbally or in writing. All complaints will be investigated promptly and confidentially.

### Corrective Action

Appropriate corrective action will be taken against any individual found to have engaged in harassment, up to and including termination.

title: Infection Control and Prevention Policy
description: Establishes standard precautions, hand hygiene, and infection prevention protocols for the practice.
bodyMarkdown:
## Infection Control and Prevention Policy

**Purpose:** To establish infection control practices that protect patients, staff, and visitors at [PRACTICE NAME].

### Standard Precautions

Standard Precautions shall be observed for all patients regardless of diagnosis or presumed infection status. This includes hand hygiene, use of PPE, respiratory hygiene, safe injection practices, and environmental cleaning.

### Hand Hygiene

Hand hygiene shall be performed: before and after patient contact, before and after performing procedures, after contact with blood/body fluids, after removing gloves, and after touching contaminated surfaces.

### Environmental Cleaning

Patient care areas shall be cleaned and disinfected between patients using EPA-registered disinfectants. High-touch surfaces require more frequent cleaning.

### Employee Health

Staff with potentially communicable infections shall not provide direct patient care until cleared by occupational health. Immunization records shall be maintained per OSHA and state requirements.

title: Medical Waste Disposal Policy
description: Procedures for proper segregation, handling, storage, and disposal of regulated medical waste.
bodyMarkdown:
## Medical Waste Disposal Policy

**Purpose:** To ensure proper handling and disposal of regulated medical waste generated at [PRACTICE NAME] in compliance with OSHA, EPA, and state regulations.

### Waste Categories

- Sharps: Needles, scalpel blades, broken glass — disposed in puncture-resistant sharps containers

- Biohazardous: Blood-soaked items, pathological waste — red bags or containers with biohazard symbol

- Pharmaceutical: Expired/unused medications — segregated per EPA and DEA requirements

- Chemical: Disinfectants, reagents — handled per SDS and hazardous waste regulations

### Storage

Medical waste shall be stored in designated, labeled, secure areas with limited access. Storage time shall not exceed [STATE LIMIT] days.

### Transport and Disposal

Regulated medical waste shall be transported and disposed of by a licensed medical waste hauler. Manifests and tracking documents shall be retained for [RETENTION PERIOD].

title: Staff Immunization and Health Screening Policy
description: Establishes requirements for workforce immunizations and health screenings.
bodyMarkdown:
## Staff Immunization and Health Screening Policy

**Purpose:** To protect patients, staff, and visitors by establishing immunization and health screening requirements for all workforce members at [PRACTICE NAME].

### Required Immunizations

All clinical staff shall maintain current immunizations as recommended by the CDC for healthcare workers, including: Hepatitis B, Influenza (annual), MMR, Varicella, Tdap, and COVID-19 (as applicable).

### Hepatitis B

Per OSHA's Bloodborne Pathogens Standard, the Hepatitis B vaccine series must be offered to all employees with potential occupational exposure, at no cost and within 10 days of assignment.

### TB Screening

All new employees shall receive baseline TB screening. Annual screening shall be conducted for staff in high-risk categories.

### Documentation

Immunization records and screening results shall be maintained in confidential employee health files separate from personnel records.

### Declinations

Employees who decline recommended immunizations must sign a declination form. Declinations shall be documented and maintained on file.

title: Patient Financial Assistance Policy
description: Defines eligibility criteria and procedures for providing financial assistance to patients.
bodyMarkdown:
## Patient Financial Assistance Policy

**Purpose:** To establish guidelines for providing financial assistance to patients who are unable to pay for services at [PRACTICE NAME].

### Eligibility

Financial assistance may be available to patients whose household income falls below [PERCENTAGE]% of the Federal Poverty Level (FPL) and who lack adequate insurance coverage.

### Application Process

Patients may request financial assistance by completing a Financial Assistance Application form. Supporting documentation (tax returns, pay stubs, insurance cards) may be required.

### Determination

Applications shall be reviewed within [N] business days. Patients shall be notified in writing of the determination, including available discount levels or payment plan options.

### Non-Discrimination

Financial assistance decisions shall not be based on race, color, national origin, disability, age, sex, or religion.

title: Prescription Monitoring Program Compliance Policy
description: Establishes procedures for compliance with state prescription drug monitoring program (PDMP) requirements.
bodyMarkdown:
## Prescription Monitoring Program Compliance Policy

**Purpose:** To ensure [PRACTICE NAME] complies with state Prescription Drug Monitoring Program (PDMP) requirements when prescribing or dispensing controlled substances.

### PDMP Query Requirements

Prescribers must query the state PDMP database before prescribing Schedule II-V controlled substances. Queries shall be documented in the patient record.

### Frequency

- Initial prescription: PDMP query required before prescribing

- Ongoing prescriptions: Query at least every [STATE REQUIREMENT] months

- Suspicion of misuse: Immediate query required

### Red Flags

Prescribers shall review PDMP results for potential red flags including: multiple prescribers, early refills, high quantities, and combinations of opioids with benzodiazepines.

### Documentation

PDMP query results and clinical decision-making related to controlled substance prescribing shall be documented in the patient record.

title: Visitor and Contractor Access Policy
description: Controls and monitors access by visitors, vendors, and contractors to protect PHI and workplace safety.
bodyMarkdown:
## Visitor and Contractor Access Policy

**Purpose:** To control and monitor access to [PRACTICE NAME] facilities by visitors, vendors, and contractors to protect PHI, patient safety, and workplace security.

### Sign-In Requirements

All visitors and contractors must sign in at the front desk, provide identification, and wear a visitor badge while on premises. Badges must be returned upon departure.

### Escort Requirements

Visitors and contractors must be escorted in clinical areas and any area where PHI may be visible or accessible.

### Vendor Access

Vendors with access to systems containing ePHI must have a current BAA on file. IT vendors must follow the practice's access control procedures and have their activities logged.

### Restricted Areas

Server rooms, medical records storage, and medication storage areas require specific authorization for access. Visitors are not permitted in these areas without prior approval.

### After-Hours Access

After-hours access by contractors requires advance approval and must be logged. The practice shall ensure appropriate supervision during after-hours work.

title: Encryption Policy
description: Defines encryption requirements for all ePHI at rest and in transit at [PRACTICE NAME].
bodyMarkdown:
## Encryption Policy

**Purpose:** To establish encryption requirements that protect electronic protected health information (ePHI) at [PRACTICE NAME] in compliance with the HIPAA Security Rule (45 CFR §164.312(a)(2)(iv) and §164.312(e)(2)(ii)).

### Scope

This policy applies to all systems, devices, applications, and communication channels that create, store, transmit, or process ePHI, including workstations, laptops, mobile devices, servers, cloud services, email, and removable media.

### Encryption Standards

- Data at Rest: All ePHI stored on servers, workstations, laptops, and portable devices must be encrypted using AES-256 or an equivalent NIST-approved algorithm. Full-disk encryption is required on all endpoints.

- Data in Transit: All ePHI transmitted over networks must use TLS 1.2 or higher. Unencrypted transmission of ePHI via email, messaging, or file transfer is prohibited.

- Backup Media: All backup copies of ePHI must be encrypted to the same standard as primary data stores.

### Key Management

Encryption keys must be generated, stored, and retired according to industry best practices. Keys shall be stored separately from the data they protect. Access to encryption keys is restricted to authorized personnel only. Key rotation shall occur at least annually or upon suspected compromise.

### Device Encryption

All laptops, tablets, smartphones, and portable devices that may contain ePHI must have device-level encryption enabled before being placed into service. Devices that cannot support encryption may not be used to access or store ePHI.

### Email Encryption

ePHI transmitted via email must use an approved encryption method. Staff shall use the practice's secure messaging system or encrypted email service when communicating ePHI externally.

### Portable Media

USB drives, external hard drives, and other removable media containing ePHI must be encrypted. Unapproved removable media may not be used for ePHI storage or transfer.

### Exceptions

Any exception to this policy must be documented with a risk assessment explaining the alternative safeguard in place. Exceptions must be approved by the Security Officer and reviewed annually.

### Review

This policy shall be reviewed annually or whenever a significant change occurs in technology, operations, or regulatory requirements. The Security Officer is responsible for ensuring ongoing compliance.

title: Multi-Factor Authentication Policy
description: Requires multi-factor authentication for all systems accessing ePHI at [PRACTICE NAME].
bodyMarkdown:
## Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) Policy

**Purpose:** To enhance the security of ePHI by requiring multi-factor authentication (MFA) for access to systems, applications, and services that store or process protected health information at [PRACTICE NAME].

### Scope

This policy applies to all workforce members, contractors, and business associates who access systems containing ePHI, including EHR systems, practice management software, email, cloud services, VPN connections, and administrative portals.

### MFA Requirement

All users must authenticate with at least two of the following factors before accessing ePHI systems:

- Something you know: Password or PIN

- Something you have: Authenticator app, hardware security key, or SMS code

- Something you are: Biometric verification (fingerprint, facial recognition)

### Approved MFA Methods

1. Authenticator Apps (preferred): Time-based one-time passwords via approved apps such as Google Authenticator or Microsoft Authenticator

2. Hardware Security Keys: FIDO2-compliant hardware tokens

3. Biometric Authentication: Device-integrated biometrics (fingerprint, face ID)

4. SMS Codes: Permitted only when other methods are unavailable; must be phased out within 12 months

### Enrollment

All workforce members must enroll in MFA within 5 business days of receiving system access. New hires must complete MFA enrollment during onboarding before accessing any ePHI system. MFA enrollment records are maintained by the Security Officer.

### Recovery Procedures

Users who lose access to their MFA device must contact the Security Officer or designated IT support for identity verification and temporary access. Recovery requires in-person or video verification of identity. Temporary bypass codes expire within 24 hours.

### Exception Process

Exceptions to MFA requirements must be submitted in writing with a justification and approved by the Security Officer. All exceptions must include compensating controls and be reviewed quarterly.

### Monitoring and Enforcement

The Security Officer shall monitor MFA compliance and generate monthly reports. Repeated failure to comply with MFA requirements may result in access suspension and disciplinary action per the Sanction Policy.

title: Technology Asset Management Policy
description: Governs inventory and lifecycle management of all technology assets at [PRACTICE NAME].
bodyMarkdown:
## Technology Asset Management Policy

**Purpose:** To establish procedures for identifying, tracking, and managing all technology assets at [PRACTICE NAME], with particular attention to assets that create, store, or transmit ePHI, as required by the HIPAA Security Rule (45 CFR §164.310(d)).

### Asset Identification and Classification

All technology assets shall be assigned a unique identifier and classified based on data sensitivity:

- ePHI Assets (Critical): Servers, workstations, laptops, mobile devices, and applications that store or process ePHI

- Infrastructure Assets (High): Network equipment, firewalls, switches, and access points

- General Assets (Standard): Printers, monitors, peripherals, and non-ePHI systems

### Asset Inventory

The Security Officer shall maintain a current inventory of all technology assets. The inventory must include: asset type, make/model, serial number, assigned user, location, ePHI classification, encryption status, and date placed into service. The inventory shall be stored securely and backed up regularly.

### Acquisition

New technology assets must be approved by the Security Officer or practice manager before purchase. All devices must meet minimum security requirements (encryption capability, current OS support, MFA compatibility) before deployment. Assets are configured according to the practice's security baseline before being placed into service.

### Disposal and Decommissioning

Assets being retired or disposed of must have all ePHI securely erased using NIST SP 800-88 compliant methods (clear, purge, or destroy). Certificates of destruction shall be obtained and retained for 6 years. Hard drives and storage media that cannot be reliably wiped must be physically destroyed.

### Annual Inventory Review

A complete physical inventory of all technology assets shall be conducted at least annually. The review shall verify that all assets are accounted for, properly classified, and meet current security standards. Discrepancies must be investigated and resolved within 30 days.

### Change Management

Changes to ePHI asset configurations, locations, or assignments must be documented and approved. Transfers of assets between users require data sanitization and reconfiguration.

### Roles and Responsibilities

The Security Officer oversees the asset management program. All staff are responsible for reporting lost, stolen, or damaged assets immediately.

title: Vulnerability Management Policy
description: Establishes vulnerability scanning and remediation requirements for ePHI systems at [PRACTICE NAME].
bodyMarkdown:
## Vulnerability Management Policy

**Purpose:** To identify, evaluate, and remediate security vulnerabilities in systems that store, process, or transmit ePHI at [PRACTICE NAME], in support of the HIPAA Security Rule requirements for technical evaluation (45 CFR §164.312(a)(1)) and security management (45 CFR §164.308(a)(1)).

### Scope

This policy applies to all information systems, network devices, applications, and services within the [PRACTICE NAME] environment that interact with ePHI, including cloud-hosted services and third-party applications.

### Vulnerability Scanning

- Frequency: Vulnerability scans shall be performed at least semi-annually (every 6 months) on all in-scope systems. Additional scans shall be conducted after significant system changes or upon discovery of new threats.

- Scope of Scanning: All servers, workstations, network devices, web applications, and cloud services that store or process ePHI must be included in regular scans.

- Tools: Scanning shall be performed using industry-recognized vulnerability assessment tools capable of detecting known CVEs and misconfigurations.

### Remediation Timelines

Identified vulnerabilities shall be remediated based on severity classification:

1. Critical: Remediate within 48 hours of discovery. If immediate remediation is not possible, implement compensating controls and document a remediation plan.

2. High: Remediate within 7 calendar days

3. Medium: Remediate within 30 calendar days

4. Low: Remediate within 90 calendar days

### Exception Process

When a vulnerability cannot be remediated within the prescribed timeline, the Security Officer must document the exception with a risk assessment, identify compensating controls, establish a revised timeline, and obtain approval from practice leadership.

### Documentation

All scan results, remediation actions, and exceptions must be documented and retained for a minimum of 6 years. Remediation activities shall be tracked to completion and verified through rescanning.

### Reporting

The Security Officer shall provide a vulnerability status report to practice leadership quarterly, summarizing open vulnerabilities, remediation progress, and overall risk posture.

title: Penetration Testing Policy
description: Defines annual penetration testing requirements for ePHI systems at [PRACTICE NAME].
bodyMarkdown:
## Penetration Testing Policy

**Purpose:** To verify the effectiveness of security controls protecting ePHI at [PRACTICE NAME] through regular penetration testing, supporting HIPAA Security Rule requirements for technical evaluation (45 CFR §164.308(a)(8)).

### Scope

Penetration testing shall cover all systems, networks, and applications that store, process, or transmit ePHI, including:

- External-facing systems (web applications, patient portals, VPN endpoints)

- Internal network infrastructure

- EHR and practice management systems

- Wireless networks

- Cloud-hosted services containing ePHI

### Frequency

Penetration testing shall be performed at least annually. Additional testing is required after significant infrastructure changes, major application deployments, or security incidents that suggest a potential compromise.

### Vendor Qualifications

Penetration testing must be performed by qualified, independent third-party security professionals who hold recognized certifications (e.g., OSCP, CEH, GPEN) and carry professional liability insurance. Testers must sign a non-disclosure agreement and BAA if they may encounter ePHI during testing.

### Rules of Engagement

Prior to testing, a written agreement shall define the scope, methods, timing, escalation procedures, and emergency contacts. Testing shall be scheduled to minimize disruption to patient care. Destructive testing or denial-of-service testing requires explicit written approval.

### Reporting

The testing vendor shall deliver a written report within 15 business days of testing completion. The report must include: executive summary, methodology, findings with severity ratings, evidence and proof-of-concept details, and prioritized remediation recommendations.

### Remediation Tracking

Critical and high-severity findings must be addressed within 30 days. The Security Officer shall track all findings to resolution. Remediation of critical findings shall be verified through targeted retesting.

### Evidence Retention

Penetration test reports, remediation records, and retesting results shall be retained for a minimum of 6 years. Reports shall be stored securely with access limited to authorized personnel.

title: Business Associate Technical Verification Policy
description: Establishes annual technical verification requirements for business associates at [PRACTICE NAME].
bodyMarkdown:
## Business Associate Technical Verification Policy

**Purpose:** To ensure that business associates (BAs) of [PRACTICE NAME] maintain adequate technical safeguards to protect ePHI, as required by the HIPAA Security Rule (45 CFR §164.314(a)) and the HITECH Act.

### Scope

This policy applies to all business associates who create, receive, maintain, or transmit ePHI on behalf of [PRACTICE NAME], including IT service providers, cloud hosting vendors, billing companies, clearinghouses, and any subcontractors with ePHI access.

### Annual Verification Process

The Privacy/Security Officer shall conduct an annual technical verification of each business associate. Verification shall be initiated no later than the anniversary of the BAA effective date. The process includes sending a standardized security questionnaire, reviewing responses, and documenting findings.

### Required Safeguards to Verify

- Encryption: Confirm ePHI is encrypted at rest and in transit using industry-standard methods

- Multi-Factor Authentication: Verify MFA is required for access to systems containing ePHI

- Audit Logging: Confirm the BA maintains audit logs of ePHI access and can produce them upon request

- Incident Response: Verify the BA has a documented incident response and breach notification procedure

- Security Risk Assessment: Confirm the BA conducts an annual SRA

- Backup and Recovery: Verify data backup procedures and disaster recovery capabilities

### Verification Documentation

All verification questionnaire responses, supporting documentation (SOC 2 reports, penetration test summaries, SRA attestations), and review notes shall be retained for a minimum of 6 years.

### Non-Responsive Business Associates

If a BA fails to respond to the verification request within 30 days, the Security Officer shall send a follow-up notice. Continued non-response after 60 days shall trigger a risk assessment and potential BAA termination review.

### Remediation of Deficiencies

When deficiencies are identified, the Security Officer shall notify the BA in writing and require a corrective action plan within 30 days. Material deficiencies that pose an immediate risk to ePHI may require suspension of ePHI sharing until resolved. All remediation activities shall be tracked to completion.

### Review

This policy shall be reviewed annually. The Security Officer shall report BA verification status to practice leadership as part of the annual security review.

title: USP 797 §21 Allergen Extract Mixing Competency Policy
description: Establishes competency requirements for staff preparing allergen extracts under USP 797 §21.
bodyMarkdown:
## USP 797 §21 Allergen Extract Mixing Competency Policy

**Purpose:** To ensure that all personnel who prepare allergen extracts at [PRACTICE NAME] demonstrate and maintain the competencies required by USP General Chapter 797, Section 21, and applicable state pharmacy board regulations.

### Scope

This policy applies to all clinical staff involved in the preparation, dilution, or handling of allergen extracts for immunotherapy, including physicians, nurses, medical assistants, and pharmacy technicians.

### Initial Competency Assessment

- Didactic Training: Complete a minimum of 4 hours of instruction covering aseptic technique, allergen extract pharmacology, dilution calculations, labeling requirements, and adverse reaction recognition

- Written Examination: Pass a written quiz with a score of 80% or higher covering USP 797 §21 requirements, dilution math, and safety protocols

- Fingertip Sampling: Successfully complete gloved fingertip sampling with zero colony-forming units (CFUs) to demonstrate proper aseptic hand hygiene

- Media Fill Test: Perform a media fill simulation demonstrating aseptic technique during extract preparation without contamination

### Ongoing Competency

All qualified personnel must complete annual revalidation including a written assessment, fingertip sampling, and direct observation of aseptic technique. Any personnel who fail revalidation must cease extract preparation until remediation is completed and a passing score is achieved.

### Documentation

The practice shall maintain records of all competency assessments, test results, remediation activities, and revalidation dates. Records shall be retained for a minimum of 3 years or as required by state law, whichever is longer.

### Non-Compliance

Personnel who do not maintain current competency documentation are prohibited from preparing allergen extracts. Supervisors are responsible for verifying competency status before assigning extract preparation duties.

### Review

This policy shall be reviewed annually and updated to reflect changes in USP standards, state regulations, or practice protocols.

title: Anaphylaxis Emergency Response Protocol
description: Defines procedures for recognizing and responding to anaphylactic reactions during immunotherapy administration.
bodyMarkdown:
## Anaphylaxis Emergency Response Protocol

**Purpose:** To establish a standardized emergency response procedure for anaphylactic reactions occurring at [PRACTICE NAME] during allergen immunotherapy administration, skin testing, or other allergen exposure events.

### Scope

This protocol applies to all clinical and administrative staff present during immunotherapy injection sessions, allergy testing procedures, and any situation where a patient or staff member exhibits signs of anaphylaxis.

### Recognition of Anaphylaxis

Staff must be trained to recognize the signs and symptoms of anaphylaxis, which may include:

- Difficulty breathing, wheezing, or stridor

- Hypotension, dizziness, or loss of consciousness

- Widespread urticaria (hives), angioedema, or flushing

- Gastrointestinal symptoms (nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramping)

- Sense of impending doom or anxiety

### Immediate Response Procedure

1. Administer epinephrine intramuscularly (1:1000, 0.3–0.5 mL for adults) in the mid-outer thigh immediately upon recognition of anaphylaxis

2. Call 911 and notify the supervising physician

3. Position the patient supine with legs elevated unless respiratory distress requires an upright position

4. Establish IV access and administer normal saline if trained and authorized

5. Monitor vital signs continuously every 2–5 minutes

6. Repeat epinephrine every 5–15 minutes if symptoms persist

7. Administer supplemental oxygen if available and indicated

### Emergency Equipment Requirements

The following equipment must be immediately accessible in all areas where immunotherapy is administered: epinephrine auto-injectors and/or drawn-up syringes, blood pressure monitor, pulse oximeter, supplemental oxygen with delivery devices, IV supplies, and a stethoscope. Equipment must be inspected monthly and documented on the emergency equipment checklist.

### Mandatory Observation Period

All patients must remain in the office for a minimum of 30 minutes following each immunotherapy injection. The observation period shall be extended to 60 minutes for patients with a history of systemic reactions or those receiving dose escalations.

### Emergency Drills

The practice shall conduct anaphylaxis emergency drills at least quarterly. All clinical staff must participate in at least two drills per year. Drills must be documented with date, participants, scenario description, response time, and areas for improvement.

### Post-Event Documentation

All anaphylactic events must be documented in the patient record and reported through the practice incident reporting system within 24 hours. Documentation must include onset time, symptoms, interventions performed, response to treatment, and disposition.

### Review

This protocol shall be reviewed annually and after every anaphylactic event. Updates shall incorporate lessons learned from drills and actual events.

title: Massachusetts Written Information Security Program (WISP)
description: Comprehensive written information security program required by Massachusetts law for any entity holding personal information of MA residents.
bodyMarkdown:
## Massachusetts Written Information Security Program (WISP)

**Practice:** {{practiceName}}

**Address:** {{address}}, {{city}}, {{state}} {{zip}}

**Effective Date:** [EFFECTIVE DATE]

### 1. Purpose

This Written Information Security Program (WISP) is established pursuant to Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93H and 201 CMR 17.00 to set forth administrative, technical, and physical safeguards for the protection of personal information of residents of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts. {{practiceName}} is committed to maintaining the security and confidentiality of personal information, including protected health information (PHI), in compliance with both state and federal requirements.

### 2. Scope

This program applies to all workforce members of {{practiceName}}, including employees, contractors, volunteers, and any third party who accesses, collects, stores, uses, transmits, or disposes of personal information of Massachusetts residents. Personal information under 201 CMR 17.03 includes a resident's first and last name (or first initial and last name) in combination with a Social Security number, driver's license number, state identification number, financial account number, or credit/debit card number.

### 3. Designated Security Coordinator

{{practiceName}} designates the Privacy and Security Officer as the individual responsible for maintaining, updating, and enforcing this WISP. The Security Coordinator shall:

- Conduct an initial and annual risk assessment of internal and external threats to personal information

- Evaluate the effectiveness of current safeguards and update them as necessary

- Ensure workforce training on this WISP at hire and annually thereafter

- Document and investigate any suspected or confirmed security breaches

- Report to practice leadership on WISP compliance status at least quarterly

### 4. Administrative Safeguards

4.1. **Risk Assessment:** {{practiceName}} shall conduct a comprehensive risk assessment at least annually to identify reasonably foreseeable internal and external risks to personal information security, assess the likelihood and potential damage of identified threats, and evaluate the sufficiency of existing policies and safeguards.

4.2. **Access Control:** Access to personal information is restricted to workforce members who require such access to perform their job functions. Access privileges shall be reviewed upon hire, role change, and termination. Terminated employee access must be revoked within 24 hours of separation.

4.3. **Workforce Training:** All workforce members shall receive training on this WISP within 30 days of hire and at least annually thereafter. Training shall cover data classification, acceptable use, incident reporting procedures, and disciplinary consequences for policy violations.

4.4. **Vendor Management:** Third-party service providers with access to personal information must contractually agree to maintain appropriate security measures consistent with 201 CMR 17.00. Contracts shall require prompt breach notification to {{practiceName}}.

### 5. Technical Safeguards

5.1. **Encryption:** All personal information transmitted across public networks or stored on portable devices shall be encrypted using industry-standard encryption protocols (AES-256 or equivalent).

5.2. **Authentication:** Unique user identifiers and strong passwords are required for all systems containing personal information. Passwords must be at least 12 characters, changed every 90 days, and must not be reused within 12 cycles. Multi-factor authentication shall be implemented where technically feasible.

5.3. **Monitoring:** {{practiceName}} shall maintain reasonably up-to-date firewall protection, operating system patches, anti-malware software, and intrusion detection systems on all systems storing personal information. Audit logs shall be maintained for a minimum of six years.

### 6. Physical Safeguards

Physical access to records containing personal information shall be restricted to authorized personnel. Paper records must be stored in locked facilities or cabinets. Visitors to areas containing personal information must be escorted and logged. Disposal of physical records shall be by shredding or incineration.

### 7. Breach Response

In the event of a breach of security involving personal information, {{practiceName}} shall: (a) notify the Massachusetts Attorney General and the Office of Consumer Affairs and Business Regulation as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay, per Mass. Gen. Laws ch. 93H, § 3; (b) provide written notice to affected Massachusetts residents; and (c) document the incident, remediation steps, and outcomes. The Security Coordinator shall lead breach response efforts.

### 8. Record Retention

This WISP and all related documentation, including risk assessments, training records, and breach reports, shall be retained for a minimum of six years as required by HIPAA and state law.

### 9. Review and Updates

This WISP shall be reviewed and updated at least annually, or whenever there is a material change in business practices, technology, or applicable law. All updates shall be documented and communicated to the workforce.

### 10. Compliance and Enforcement

Violations of this WISP may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination. {{practiceName}} reserves the right to audit compliance at any time. Questions regarding this program should be directed to the Privacy and Security Officer at {{phone}}.

title: California Medical Information Act (CMIA) Authorization Policy
description: Policy governing the use and disclosure of medical information under the California Confidentiality of Medical Information Act.
bodyMarkdown:
## California Medical Information Act (CMIA) Authorization Policy

**Practice:** {{practiceName}}

**Address:** {{address}}, {{city}}, CA {{zip}}

**Effective Date:** [EFFECTIVE DATE]

### 1. Purpose

This policy establishes the requirements for obtaining, using, and disclosing medical information in compliance with the California Confidentiality of Medical Information Act (CMIA), Cal. Civ. Code §§ 56-56.37. The CMIA provides protections for medical information that in many cases exceed those required by HIPAA, and {{practiceName}} shall comply with the more stringent standard.

### 2. Scope

This policy applies to all workforce members, contractors, and business associates of {{practiceName}} who create, receive, maintain, or transmit medical information of patients. Medical information under the CMIA includes any individually identifiable information in possession of or derived from a provider of health care regarding a patient's medical history, mental or physical condition, or treatment.

### 3. Authorization Requirements

3.1. **Written Authorization:** No provider of health care, health care service plan, or contractor shall disclose medical information regarding a patient without first obtaining a valid written authorization from the patient or the patient's legal representative, except as permitted by Cal. Civ. Code § 56.10.

3.2. **Authorization Form Elements:** A valid CMIA authorization must include: (a) the specific uses and limitations on the types of medical information to be disclosed; (b) the name or functions of the persons authorized to make the disclosure; (c) the name or functions of the persons authorized to receive the disclosure; (d) the specific uses and limitations on the use of the information by the recipient; (e) a specific date after which the authorization expires; and (f) the patient's signature and date.

3.3. **Prohibitions:** Authorization forms shall not include a waiver of the patient's rights under the CMIA. Blanket or open-ended authorizations are not valid. An authorization for the release of medical information may not be combined with an authorization for the release of psychotherapy notes.

### 4. Permitted Disclosures Without Authorization

Medical information may be disclosed without patient authorization only in the circumstances enumerated in Cal. Civ. Code § 56.10(c), including but not limited to:

- To providers of health care, health care service plans, or contractors for purposes of diagnosis or treatment of the patient

- To an insurer, employer, or other person responsible for paying for health care services rendered, to the extent necessary to allow payment

- As required by a court order or subpoena

- To a county coroner or medical examiner in the course of an investigation

- When otherwise specifically required by law

### 5. Patient Rights

5.1. Patients have the right to inspect and copy their medical records within 15 days of a written request per Cal. Health & Safety Code § 123110.

5.2. Patients may revoke an authorization at any time in writing, and revocation shall be effective immediately upon receipt by {{practiceName}}, except to the extent that action has already been taken in reliance on the authorization.

5.3. {{practiceName}} shall not condition treatment or payment on the patient's signing an authorization, except where the disclosure is necessary for treatment or enrollment.

### 6. Marketing and Fundraising

Use or disclosure of medical information for marketing purposes requires a separate, specific authorization from the patient per Cal. Civ. Code § 56.10(d). Marketing does not include communications about the patient's current treatment, case management, or care coordination.

### 7. Electronic Health Records

When medical information is maintained in electronic form, {{practiceName}} shall comply with Cal. Civ. Code § 56.101, which requires that any agreement for electronic storage or maintenance of medical information include provisions for confidentiality protections at least as stringent as those imposed by the CMIA.

### 8. Enforcement and Penalties

Violations of the CMIA may result in civil liability including compensatory damages, punitive damages of up to $3,000, attorney fees, and costs of litigation per Cal. Civ. Code § 56.35-56.36. Workforce members who violate this policy are subject to disciplinary action up to and including termination.

### 9. Responsibilities

The Privacy Officer is responsible for ensuring this policy is implemented, that authorization forms meet CMIA requirements, and that workforce members receive training on CMIA obligations. All workforce members are responsible for complying with this policy and reporting suspected violations to the Privacy Officer at {{phone}}.

title: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Patient Notice
description: Notice informing California patients of their rights under the CCPA regarding personal information collected outside of treatment contexts.
bodyMarkdown:
## California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Patient Notice

**Practice:** {{practiceName}}

**Address:** {{address}}, {{city}}, CA {{zip}}

**Effective Date:** [EFFECTIVE DATE]

### 1. Purpose

This notice is provided to patients and consumers in compliance with the California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA), as amended by the California Privacy Rights Act of 2020 (CPRA), Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1798.100-1798.199.100. While medical information governed by CMIA and HIPAA is exempt from the CCPA, {{practiceName}} may collect personal information outside these contexts (e.g., website usage, scheduling applications, marketing interactions) that falls under CCPA jurisdiction.

### 2. Categories of Personal Information Collected

{{practiceName}} may collect the following categories of personal information from California consumers:

- Identifiers: Name, postal address, email address, phone number, IP address

- Commercial Information: Records of services purchased, payment history

- Internet Activity: Browsing history on our website, search history, interactions with our online patient portal

- Geolocation Data: Approximate location derived from IP address

- Professional Information: Employment information provided for insurance purposes

### 3. Purposes for Collection

Personal information is collected and used for: scheduling and appointment management; billing and payment processing; communications regarding your care; quality improvement and patient satisfaction surveys; compliance with legal and regulatory obligations; and website analytics and improvement.

### 4. Consumer Rights Under the CCPA

California consumers have the following rights regarding their personal information:

4.1. **Right to Know:** You may request that {{practiceName}} disclose the categories and specific pieces of personal information collected about you, the categories of sources, the business purposes for collection, and the categories of third parties with whom information is shared.

4.2. **Right to Delete:** You may request deletion of personal information collected about you, subject to certain exceptions (e.g., completing a transaction, legal obligations, internal uses consistent with the context of collection).

4.3. **Right to Correct:** You may request correction of inaccurate personal information maintained by {{practiceName}}.

4.4. **Right to Opt-Out of Sale/Sharing:** {{practiceName}} does not sell personal information and does not share personal information for cross-context behavioral advertising purposes.

4.5. **Right to Non-Discrimination:** {{practiceName}} will not discriminate against you for exercising any of your CCPA rights.

### 5. Submitting Requests

To exercise your CCPA rights, contact {{practiceName}} by phone at {{phone}} or by mail at {{address}}, {{city}}, CA {{zip}}. {{practiceName}} shall respond to verifiable consumer requests within 45 calendar days of receipt. We may require identity verification before processing a request.

### 6. Data Retention

Personal information is retained only as long as necessary to fulfill the purposes for which it was collected, or as required by applicable law. Medical records are retained per California medical record retention requirements (minimum 7 years from last date of service for adults, until age 19 for minors).

### 7. Updates to This Notice

{{practiceName}} may update this notice periodically to reflect changes in our practices or applicable law. The effective date at the top of this notice indicates when it was last revised. Patients will be notified of material changes.

### 8. Contact Information

For questions about this notice or to exercise your CCPA rights, contact the Privacy Officer at {{practiceName}}, {{address}}, {{city}}, CA {{zip}}, or call {{phone}}.

title: Texas HB 300 Workforce Training Policy
description: Policy establishing workforce training requirements for protected health information under Texas Health and Safety Code Chapter 181.
bodyMarkdown:
## Texas HB 300 Workforce Training Policy

**Practice:** {{practiceName}}

**Address:** {{address}}, {{city}}, TX {{zip}}

**Effective Date:** [EFFECTIVE DATE]

### 1. Purpose

This policy establishes the mandatory training requirements for all workforce members regarding the use, access, and disclosure of protected health information (PHI) as required by Texas House Bill 300, codified in the Texas Health and Safety Code Chapter 181. Texas law requires training that goes beyond HIPAA by mandating initial and ongoing education on both state and federal privacy requirements.

### 2. Scope

This policy applies to all employees, volunteers, trainees, contractors, and other persons whose conduct in the performance of work for {{practiceName}} is under its direct control, regardless of whether they are paid by the practice. Covered entities and their employees who access, use, or disclose PHI must complete training per Tex. Health & Safety Code § 181.101.

### 3. Training Requirements

3.1. **Initial Training:** All new workforce members must complete Texas HB 300 privacy training within the first 60 days of employment or engagement with {{practiceName}}. No workforce member shall access PHI until initial training is completed, except under direct supervision of a trained individual.

3.2. **Content Requirements:** Training must cover at a minimum: (a) the provisions of Texas Health and Safety Code Chapter 181; (b) the HIPAA Privacy Rule (45 CFR Part 164, Subpart E); (c) the practice's internal privacy policies and procedures; (d) the proper handling, access, use, and disclosure of PHI; (e) patient rights under Texas and federal law; (f) the consequences of violating privacy requirements, including civil and criminal penalties.

3.3. **Ongoing Training:** Refresher training shall be completed at least every two years as required by Tex. Health & Safety Code § 181.101(b). Additional training is required when there are material changes to privacy laws or the practice's privacy policies.

3.4. **Training Format:** Training may be provided in person, via online learning management systems, or through a combination of methods. Interactive components and competency assessments are required to verify understanding.

### 4. Documentation

{{practiceName}} shall maintain documentation of all training activities, including: the date of training, the content covered, the names of attendees, the name of the trainer or training program used, and the results of any competency assessments. Training records shall be retained for a minimum of six years from the date of training.

### 5. Penalties for Non-Compliance

Under Texas HB 300, violations of PHI privacy requirements can result in civil penalties of $5,000 to $250,000 per violation. The Texas Attorney General has enforcement authority. Additionally, individuals who access PHI without authorization may face criminal penalties under Tex. Health & Safety Code § 181.201, including fines and imprisonment.

### 6. Employee Acknowledgment

All workforce members must sign an acknowledgment form confirming they have completed the required training and understand their obligations regarding PHI under both Texas and federal law. Failure to complete required training within the specified timeframe is grounds for suspension of system access and may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination.

### 7. Responsibilities

The Privacy Officer is responsible for ensuring that training curricula meet the requirements of Texas HB 300, scheduling and tracking completion, and maintaining training records. Department supervisors are responsible for ensuring their team members complete training within required timeframes. Questions about this policy should be directed to the Privacy Officer at {{phone}}.

title: Texas HB 300 Marketing Authorization Policy
description: Policy governing the use of PHI for marketing purposes under the Texas Health and Safety Code requirements that exceed HIPAA.
bodyMarkdown:
## Texas HB 300 Marketing Authorization Policy

**Practice:** {{practiceName}}

**Address:** {{address}}, {{city}}, TX {{zip}}

**Effective Date:** [EFFECTIVE DATE]

### 1. Purpose

This policy establishes requirements for obtaining patient authorization before using protected health information (PHI) for marketing purposes, as mandated by Texas Health and Safety Code § 181.152. Texas HB 300 imposes restrictions on the use of PHI for marketing that exceed HIPAA's requirements, including a prohibition on the sale of PHI and stricter consent requirements for electronic marketing communications.

### 2. Scope

This policy applies to all workforce members, departments, and business associates of {{practiceName}} who engage in or support marketing activities that involve the use of patient health information. Marketing includes any communication about a product or service that encourages recipients to purchase or use the product or service, regardless of the medium.

### 3. Definitions

3.1. **Marketing:** A communication about a product or service that encourages recipients to purchase or use the product or service, excluding: refill reminders, communications about currently prescribed medications, treatment alternatives, health-related products or services offered by {{practiceName}}, and case management or care coordination.

3.2. **Sale of PHI:** Any disclosure of PHI by {{practiceName}} where the practice receives direct or indirect remuneration from the entity receiving the PHI. Texas law strictly prohibits the sale of PHI per Tex. Health & Safety Code § 181.153.

### 4. Authorization Requirements

4.1. Prior to using PHI for marketing communications, {{practiceName}} must obtain a signed authorization from the patient that: (a) clearly states the marketing purpose; (b) identifies whether {{practiceName}} will receive remuneration from a third party in connection with the communication; (c) specifies the types of PHI to be used; (d) allows the patient to opt out at any time; and (e) includes an expiration date.

4.2. **Electronic Communications:** Marketing via email, text message, or other electronic means requires explicit opt-in consent in addition to the HIPAA/HB 300 authorization. Patients must be provided a clear and conspicuous method to opt out of electronic marketing at any time.

4.3. **Prohibition on Sale:** {{practiceName}} shall not sell PHI under any circumstances. Any arrangement that involves direct or indirect remuneration for the disclosure of PHI must be reviewed by the Privacy Officer and legal counsel before execution.

### 5. Permitted Communications

The following communications are not considered marketing and do not require a marketing authorization: communications for treatment purposes, appointment reminders, prescription refill reminders, communications about treatment alternatives or health-related benefits available to the patient, general health promotion materials not targeted based on health conditions, and communications required by law.

### 6. Third-Party Marketing Agreements

Any agreement with a third party that involves the use of patient information for marketing must be reviewed and approved by the Privacy Officer. Business associate agreements must include provisions addressing Texas HB 300 marketing restrictions. Third parties must agree not to use PHI beyond the scope authorized by the patient.

### 7. Recordkeeping

{{practiceName}} shall maintain copies of all marketing authorizations, opt-in records for electronic marketing, and documentation of marketing-related disclosures for a minimum of six years. Records must include the date of authorization, the scope of the authorization, and the date of any revocation.

### 8. Enforcement

Violations of this policy may result in penalties under Tex. Health & Safety Code § 181.201 (civil penalties of $5,000 to $250,000 per violation) and disciplinary action up to and including termination. The Texas Attorney General may bring enforcement actions for violations. Reports of suspected violations should be directed to the Privacy Officer at {{phone}}.

title: New York SHIELD Act Data Security Policy
description: Data security policy implementing the requirements of the New York Stop Hacks and Improve Electronic Data Security Act.
bodyMarkdown:
## New York SHIELD Act Data Security Policy

**Practice:** {{practiceName}}

**Address:** {{address}}, {{city}}, NY {{zip}}

**Effective Date:** [EFFECTIVE DATE]

### 1. Purpose

This policy implements the data security requirements of the New York Stop Hacks and Improve Electronic Data Security (SHIELD) Act, N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law § 899-bb, effective March 21, 2020. The SHIELD Act requires businesses that hold private information of New York residents to implement and maintain reasonable safeguards to protect the security, confidentiality, and integrity of that information.

### 2. Scope

This policy applies to all workforce members of {{practiceName}} and covers all private information of New York residents that is owned or licensed by the practice. Private information includes a personal name plus any of the following: Social Security number, driver's license or non-driver ID number, account number with security code or password, biometric information, or a username/email with password or security question answers.

### 3. Administrative Safeguards

{{practiceName}} shall implement the following administrative safeguards as required by the SHIELD Act:

- Designate one or more employees to coordinate the security program (Privacy and Security Officer)

- Identify reasonably foreseeable internal and external risks to the security of private information

- Assess the sufficiency of safeguards in place to control identified risks

- Train and manage workforce members in security program practices and procedures

- Select service providers capable of maintaining appropriate safeguards and require them by contract to do so

- Adjust the security program in light of business changes or new circumstances

### 4. Technical Safeguards

{{practiceName}} shall implement the following technical safeguards:

- Assess risks in network and software design and information processing, transmission, and storage

- Detect, prevent, and respond to attacks or system failures through intrusion detection, firewalls, and endpoint protection

- Regularly test and monitor the effectiveness of key controls, systems, and procedures through vulnerability assessments and penetration testing

- Encrypt private information transmitted over public networks and stored on portable devices

- Implement multi-factor authentication for remote access to systems containing private information

### 5. Physical Safeguards

{{practiceName}} shall implement the following physical safeguards:

- Assess risks of information storage and disposal, including whether records are stored in locked areas

- Detect, prevent, and respond to intrusions through visitor logs, access controls, and physical security monitoring

- Protect against unauthorized access to or use of private information during or after collection, transportation, and destruction

- Dispose of private information within a reasonable amount of time after it is no longer needed by cross-cut shredding, degaussing, or destroying the media on which it is stored

### 6. Breach Notification Requirements

Under the SHIELD Act (N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law § 899-aa), {{practiceName}} must notify affected New York residents in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay when a breach of private information is discovered. Notification must also be provided to the New York Attorney General, the Department of State, and the Division of State Police if more than 500 residents are affected. The notification must include a description of the breach, the categories of information compromised, and contact information for the practice.

### 7. Compliance Verification

The Security Officer shall conduct annual assessments of this security program, document findings, and implement corrective actions for any deficiencies. Assessment results and corrective action plans shall be reported to practice leadership and retained for six years.

### 8. Enforcement

The New York Attorney General may bring enforcement actions for violations of the SHIELD Act. Penalties include injunctive relief and civil penalties. Workforce members who violate this policy are subject to disciplinary action. Contact the Security Officer at {{phone}} with questions.

title: New York Patient Record Access Procedure (10-Day Rule)
description: Procedure for providing patients access to medical records within 10 days as required by New York Public Health Law.
bodyMarkdown:
## New York Patient Record Access Procedure (10-Day Rule)

**Practice:** {{practiceName}}

**Address:** {{address}}, {{city}}, NY {{zip}}

**Effective Date:** [EFFECTIVE DATE]

### 1. Purpose

This procedure establishes the requirements and timeline for providing patients with access to their medical records in compliance with New York Public Health Law § 18, which mandates a stricter timeline than HIPAA. While HIPAA allows up to 30 days (with a 30-day extension) to respond to a record access request, New York law requires a response within 10 days of the request.

### 2. Scope

This procedure applies to all workforce members of {{practiceName}} who receive, process, or fulfill patient requests for access to medical records. It covers all patient health information maintained by the practice, regardless of format (paper, electronic, images, lab results).

### 3. Timeline Requirements

3.1. **10-Day Rule:** Upon receipt of a written request from a qualified person (patient, patient's representative, or authorized third party), {{practiceName}} must provide access to or copies of the requested medical records within 10 days per N.Y. Pub. Health Law § 18(2)(e).

3.2. **Receipt Date:** The 10-day clock begins on the date the written request is received by any workforce member of {{practiceName}}, not the date it reaches the medical records department. All workforce members must immediately forward record requests to the designated records coordinator.

3.3. **No Extension:** Unlike HIPAA's 30-day extension provision, New York law does not provide for an extension of the 10-day deadline. If records cannot be provided within 10 days, {{practiceName}} must inform the patient in writing of the reason and the expected date of fulfillment.

### 4. Request Processing Procedure

4.1. **Step 1 — Intake:** Log the request immediately upon receipt, recording: patient name, date of birth, date request received, specific records requested, requester identity and relationship to patient, and preferred delivery method.

4.2. **Step 2 — Verification:** Verify the identity of the requester and their authority to access the records. For third-party requests, verify valid written authorization from the patient. Verification must be completed within 2 business days of receipt.

4.3. **Step 3 — Compilation:** Compile the requested records, including all documents within the scope of the request. Review for completeness and ensure no records of other patients are inadvertently included.

4.4. **Step 4 — Review:** A qualified provider may review the records to determine if any information should be withheld under the limited exceptions in N.Y. Pub. Health Law § 18(3) (e.g., information that would cause substantial and identifiable harm to the patient or others).

4.5. **Step 5 — Delivery:** Provide the records in the format requested by the patient (paper, electronic, or both). If electronic records are requested, provide them in a machine-readable format if readily producible.

### 5. Fees

Per N.Y. Pub. Health Law § 18(2)(e), {{practiceName}} may charge a reasonable fee for copies not to exceed $0.75 per page. No fee may be charged for records requested for the purpose of supporting a claim or appeal for any government benefit or program. The fee for electronic copies shall not exceed the cost of the media and labor for creating the copy.

### 6. Denials

Access may be denied only in the limited circumstances specified in N.Y. Pub. Health Law § 18(3). If any portion of a request is denied, the patient must be informed in writing of: the reason for the denial, the patient's right to have the denial reviewed by the practice, and the patient's right to seek court review. Partial denials must still result in disclosure of all non-exempt portions of the record within the 10-day timeframe.

### 7. Documentation and Tracking

The records coordinator shall maintain a log of all record requests including the date received, date fulfilled, any fees charged, and any denials. This log shall be reviewed monthly to ensure compliance with the 10-day timeline. Patterns of delay must be reported to practice leadership for corrective action.

### 8. Responsibilities

The Records Coordinator is responsible for processing all record requests within the required timeline. All workforce members are responsible for immediately forwarding any record requests to the Records Coordinator. The Privacy Officer oversees compliance with this procedure. Contact the Privacy Officer at {{phone}} with questions.

title: Florida FIPA Breach Response Addendum
description: Addendum to breach response procedures addressing Florida Information Protection Act requirements including the 30-day notification timeline.
bodyMarkdown:
## Florida FIPA Breach Response Addendum

**Practice:** {{practiceName}}

**Address:** {{address}}, {{city}}, FL {{zip}}

**Effective Date:** [EFFECTIVE DATE]

### 1. Purpose

This addendum supplements the practice's existing breach response and notification procedures to ensure compliance with the Florida Information Protection Act of 2014 (FIPA), Fla. Stat. § 501.171. FIPA imposes breach notification requirements that differ from HIPAA in key respects, including a shorter notification timeline and broader definition of personal information. {{practiceName}} must comply with both HIPAA and FIPA when a breach involves Florida residents.

### 2. Scope

This addendum applies whenever a breach of security involves personal information of Florida residents as defined by FIPA. Personal information under FIPA includes a person's first name or initial and last name in combination with: Social Security number, driver's license or state ID number, passport number, military ID number, financial account number with required access credentials, medical history or mental/physical condition information, or health insurance policy or subscriber ID numbers.

### 3. Notification Timeline

3.1. **30-Day Requirement:** FIPA requires notification to affected Florida residents no later than 30 days after determination of a breach or reason to believe a breach occurred, per Fla. Stat. § 501.171(4)(a). This is stricter than HIPAA's 60-day requirement.

3.2. **Law Enforcement Delay:** Notification may be delayed if a law enforcement agency determines that notification would interfere with a criminal investigation. The delay must be requested in writing by law enforcement and is limited to a reasonable period.

3.3. **Attorney General Notification:** If the breach affects 500 or more Florida residents, {{practiceName}} must notify the Florida Department of Legal Affairs (Attorney General) within 30 days of breach determination per Fla. Stat. § 501.171(3).

### 4. Notification Content

Written notice to affected individuals must include:

- The date, estimated date, or estimated date range of the breach

- A description of the personal information that was accessed or reasonably believed to have been accessed

- Contact information for {{practiceName}}, including a toll-free phone number if available, mailing address, and email address

- A statement that the individual can obtain information about identity theft prevention and protection from the Federal Trade Commission and the Florida Attorney General

### 5. Notification Methods

5.1. **Written Notice:** Sent by first-class mail to the last known address of the affected individual.

5.2. **Email Notice:** May be used if the individual has previously consented to electronic communication and the notice is consistent with the E-SIGN Act (15 U.S.C. § 7001).
